items.title: ASP.NET Core IIS deployment failure (site loads locally, fails under IIS)
items.description: Symptoms: the app runs under 'dotnet run' / Kestrel but returns 500.x or a blank page once published to IIS. Root cause: the published output is missing the framework-dependent runtime, the web.config aspNetCore handler points at the wrong processPath/arguments, or the application physical path points at the source folder rather than the publish output. Diagnostic: dotnet publish -c Release -o ./publish, then confirm web.config has an aspNetCore element and the publish folder contains <App>.dll and appsettings.json. Known fix: deploy the publish output (not the project folder), install the matching ASP.NET Core Hosting Bundle, and set the IIS site physical path to the publish directory. Prevention rule: always deploy a clean publish output and pin the targeted runtime version. LocalAIFactory detection: when a deploy job reports 500.x and Kestrel was healthy, flag 'IIS hosting layer, not app code'. Fix/warn: warn the operator to verify Hosting Bundle + web.config + physical path before any code change.
items.applicability: Any ASP.NET Core app published to IIS on Windows Server / IIS Express, especially first deployments and version upgrades.
items.example: Site returns 500.30 in browser but /publish/App.dll runs fine via 'dotnet App.dll' from the publish folder, isolating the fault to IIS configuration.
items.limitation: Does not cover load-balancer or reverse-proxy layers in front of IIS; those add their own failure modes.
items.symptoms: 500.x or blank page under IIS while Kestrel runs cleanly.
items.rootCause: Wrong physical path, missing runtime/hosting bundle, or malformed web.config aspNetCore handler.
items.diagnosticCommand: dotnet publish -c Release -o ./publish ; then run 'dotnet ./publish/App.dll' to confirm the app itself is healthy.
items.knownFix: Deploy publish output, install matching Hosting Bundle, point IIS physical path at the publish folder.
items.preventionRule: Never point IIS at the source tree; always deploy a fresh publish output with a pinned runtime.
items.detectBy: Deploy job: 500.x under IIS but Kestrel probe succeeded.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: check Hosting Bundle, web.config, and physical path before editing code.

items.title: ANCM / ASP.NET Core Hosting Bundle missing or mismatched
items.description: Symptoms: IIS logs reference the ASP.NET Core Module (ANCM) failing to start the process, or the aspNetCore handler is not registered. Root cause: the ASP.NET Core Hosting Bundle is absent, an older major version than the app's target, or IIS was not restarted (iisreset) after installing it. Firsthand finding in this project: a freshly imaged IIS host had no ANCM at all until the Hosting Bundle was installed and IIS restarted; publishing alone did not register the module. Diagnostic: check %ProgramFiles%/IIS/Asp.Net Core Module and run 'dotnet --list-runtimes' to confirm the ASP.NET Core shared framework is present and matches the target. Known fix: install the Hosting Bundle matching the app's major.minor, then iisreset. Prevention rule: bundle the Hosting Bundle install + iisreset into the deployment script. LocalAIFactory detection: ANCM-related event entries or a missing module. Fix/warn: instruct to install the matching Hosting Bundle and run iisreset, not to recompile.
items.applicability: Windows hosts that have IIS but have never hosted an ASP.NET Core app, or after a major .NET upgrade.
items.example: After OS imaging, IIS had no ANCM; installing the Hosting Bundle and running iisreset restored process startup.
items.limitation: Version matching is approximate across patch levels; verify exact runtime requirements against the deployed framework.
items.symptoms: ANCM start failure or aspNetCore handler not registered in IIS.
items.rootCause: Hosting Bundle missing, too old, or installed without iisreset.
items.diagnosticCommand: dotnet --list-runtimes ; check for the ASP.NET Core Module under IIS native modules.
items.knownFix: Install matching ASP.NET Core Hosting Bundle, then iisreset.
items.preventionRule: Script Hosting Bundle install + iisreset into deployment; never assume a fresh host has ANCM.
items.detectBy: Event log/IIS references to ANCM start failure or missing module.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: install Hosting Bundle + iisreset rather than changing app code.

items.title: IIS Windows Authentication (Negotiate/NTLM) failure
items.description: Symptoms: repeated 401 challenges, browser prompts that never succeed, or anonymous identity where a domain user was expected. Root cause: Windows Authentication is not enabled on the site, Anonymous auth is left on and shadows it, the app does not request the Negotiate scheme, or an SPN/Kerberos delegation gap forces a fallback that fails. Diagnostic: confirm the Windows Authentication IIS role feature is installed, that the site has Windows auth enabled and Anonymous disabled, and inspect the WWW-Authenticate header. Known fix: enable Windows Authentication, disable Anonymous, register Negotiate in the app, and ensure the app pool identity can use the SPN. Prevention rule: keep auth configuration in deployment automation and test with a real domain account. LocalAIFactory detection: 401 loops with Negotiate present in headers. Fix/warn: warn that this is an IIS+AD auth wiring issue, not an app authorization-policy bug.
items.applicability: Intranet apps relying on integrated Windows auth behind IIS in a domain.
items.example: A browser keeps prompting; enabling Windows auth and disabling Anonymous on the IIS site stops the 401 loop.
items.limitation: Kerberos vs NTLM delegation specifics depend on AD/SPN configuration outside the app.
items.symptoms: 401 loops, endless credential prompts, or unexpected anonymous identity.
items.rootCause: Windows auth disabled, Anonymous enabled, missing Negotiate scheme, or SPN/Kerberos gap.
items.diagnosticCommand: Inspect WWW-Authenticate response header; confirm IIS Windows-Authentication feature and site auth settings.
items.knownFix: Enable Windows auth, disable Anonymous, register Negotiate, verify app-pool SPN.
items.preventionRule: Automate auth config and test with a domain account before release.
items.detectBy: Repeated 401 with Negotiate/NTLM in headers and no successful token.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: IIS/AD auth wiring, not an app policy bug.

items.title: SQL Server Express connection failure
items.description: Symptoms: 'A network-related or instance-specific error', login timeouts, or 'server not found' when connecting to SQL Express. Root cause: the named instance (e.g. .\SQLEXPRESS) is wrong, the SQL Browser service is stopped so the dynamic port cannot be resolved, TCP/IP is disabled in SQL Server Configuration Manager, or the firewall blocks the port. Diagnostic: sqlcmd -S .\SQLEXPRESS -E -Q "SELECT @@VERSION" to test reachability and auth in isolation. Known fix: start the SQL Browser service (or use a static port), enable TCP/IP, open the firewall, and correct the instance name in the connection string. Prevention rule: standardize the instance name and document the connection string in environment config. LocalAIFactory detection: startup migration fails with a SQL connection error. Fix/warn: warn to verify instance name, SQL Browser, TCP/IP, and firewall before assuming an app/config fault.
items.applicability: Any deployment using SQL Server Express as the primary store, especially first run on a new machine.
items.example: App startup migration throws a network error; starting SQL Browser and enabling TCP/IP resolves it.
items.limitation: Does not cover Always On / clustered SQL topologies.
items.symptoms: Network-related error, login timeout, or instance not found connecting to SQL Express.
items.rootCause: Wrong instance name, SQL Browser stopped, TCP/IP disabled, or firewall block.
items.diagnosticCommand: sqlcmd -S .\SQLEXPRESS -E -Q "SELECT @@VERSION"
items.knownFix: Start SQL Browser (or static port), enable TCP/IP, open firewall, fix instance name in connection string.
items.preventionRule: Standardize instance name; keep the connection string in environment config.
items.detectBy: Startup migration or first query fails with a SQL network/connection error.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: verify instance name, SQL Browser, TCP/IP, firewall before touching app code.

items.title: EF Core migration failure on startup
items.description: Symptoms: app fails to start with a pending-migration error, 'table already exists', or a snapshot/model mismatch. Root cause: migrations were not applied, the ModelSnapshot is out of sync with the migration files, a destructive change was attempted, or two heads exist after a merge. Diagnostic: dotnet ef migrations list and dotnet ef database update against the startup project to see applied vs pending. Known fix: apply pending migrations, regenerate the snapshot through EF (never hand-edit), and resolve duplicate heads. Prevention rule: keep migrations additive and the snapshot generated by EF; never edit the snapshot by hand. LocalAIFactory detection: startup migration step throws. Fix/warn: warn to inspect migrations list and snapshot consistency before adding a new migration; refuse destructive changes without approval.
items.applicability: Any EF Core app that auto-migrates on startup, especially after branch merges that touch the Data project.
items.example: Startup reports a pending migration; running database update against the Web startup project applies it cleanly.
items.limitation: Lossy/destructive migrations need explicit approval and are out of scope for automatic fixing.
items.symptoms: Pending-migration error, 'table already exists', or snapshot/model mismatch at startup.
items.rootCause: Unapplied migrations, snapshot drift, destructive change, or duplicate migration heads.
items.diagnosticCommand: dotnet ef migrations list --project src/Data --startup-project src/Web ; dotnet ef database update
items.knownFix: Apply pending migrations, regenerate snapshot via EF, reconcile duplicate heads.
items.preventionRule: Keep migrations additive; always regenerate the snapshot through EF, never by hand.
items.detectBy: Startup auto-migrate step throws a migration/snapshot error.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: inspect migrations list + snapshot before adding migrations; block destructive changes.

items.title: App-pool identity / SQL permission failure
items.description: Symptoms: 'Login failed for user' naming the machine account (DOMAIN\MACHINE$ or IIS APPPOOL\name), or permission-denied on tables despite the app working under a developer login. Root cause: the IIS app pool runs as ApplicationPoolIdentity / a service account that has no SQL login or insufficient database role. Diagnostic: read the SQL error to see which principal was rejected, then check that principal exists as a SQL login mapped to the database with the needed (least-privilege) role. Known fix: create a SQL login for the app pool identity and grant the minimum role (e.g. db_datareader/db_datawriter plus needed execute), not sysadmin. Prevention rule: provision the app-pool SQL login as part of deployment and keep is_sysadmin=0. LocalAIFactory detection: 'Login failed' naming a machine/app-pool principal. Fix/warn: warn to grant least-privilege SQL access to the app pool identity, never elevate to sysadmin.
items.applicability: IIS-hosted apps using integrated security to SQL Server with a dedicated app-pool identity.
items.example: SQL rejects 'IIS APPPOOL\LocalAIFactory'; creating that login with db_datareader/db_datawriter fixes it without elevation.
items.limitation: Exact role set depends on the app's queries and stored procedures.
items.symptoms: 'Login failed for user' for a machine/app-pool account, or permission-denied on tables.
items.rootCause: App-pool identity has no SQL login or insufficient role.
items.diagnosticCommand: Read SQL error for the rejected principal; query sys.server_principals / sys.database_principals to confirm mapping.
items.knownFix: Create a SQL login for the app-pool identity, grant least-privilege roles only.
items.preventionRule: Provision the app-pool login at deploy time; keep is_sysadmin=0.
items.detectBy: 'Login failed' error naming a machine or IIS APPPOOL principal.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: grant least-privilege SQL access; never elevate to sysadmin.

items.title: HTTPS certificate binding failure in IIS
items.description: Symptoms: HTTPS site unreachable, 'page cannot be displayed' on 443, or a cert warning/mismatch. Root cause: no HTTPS binding exists, the binding references a deleted/expired cert, the cert's private key is missing, or the hostname does not match the certificate subject/SAN. Diagnostic: netsh http show sslcert and review IIS bindings to confirm the 443 binding points at a valid cert with a private key. Known fix: import the cert (with private key) into the correct store, create/repair the 443 binding, and match the hostname to the SAN. Prevention rule: track cert expiry and automate renewal/binding. LocalAIFactory detection (pilot): self-signed cert in use; flag mismatch/expiry. Fix/warn: warn to repair the binding and verify the cert/private key rather than changing app code.
items.applicability: Any IIS site serving HTTPS, including self-signed pilot certificates.
items.example: 443 is dead because the binding referenced a removed cert; re-importing and rebinding restores HTTPS.
items.limitation: Self-signed certs still produce trust warnings on clients; not a CA-trust fix.
items.symptoms: HTTPS unreachable on 443 or certificate warning/mismatch.
items.rootCause: Missing binding, deleted/expired cert, missing private key, or hostname/SAN mismatch.
items.diagnosticCommand: netsh http show sslcert ; review IIS site bindings for the 443 entry.
items.knownFix: Import cert with private key, create/repair 443 binding, match hostname to SAN.
items.preventionRule: Track cert expiry; automate renewal and binding.
items.detectBy: 443 binding missing or pointing at an invalid/expired cert.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: repair binding and verify cert+private key, not app code.

items.title: 'netsh http add sslcert' command failure
items.description: Symptoms: 'SSL Certificate add failed, Error: 1312' (logon session does not exist), '1168' (element not found), or 'parameter is incorrect'. Root cause: a wrong certhash (with stray spaces or non-hex characters), a missing/incorrect appid GUID, the cert not present in the LocalMachine store, or a port binding already held. Diagnostic: netsh http show sslcert to inspect existing bindings, and confirm the cert thumbprint exactly (no spaces) in the Local Computer Personal store. Known fix: delete the stale binding (netsh http delete sslcert ipport=...), then add with the correct certhash and a valid appid GUID. Prevention rule: copy the thumbprint programmatically and strip whitespace. LocalAIFactory detection: netsh sslcert error codes 1312/1168. Fix/warn: warn to clean the thumbprint and verify the cert store before retrying.
items.applicability: Manual or scripted HTTP.SYS SSL bindings outside IIS Manager.
items.example: Error 1312 because the certhash had embedded spaces; stripping them and re-running succeeds.
items.limitation: appid GUID conventions vary by host app; use a stable GUID per service.
items.symptoms: netsh sslcert add fails with 1312, 1168, or 'parameter is incorrect'.
items.rootCause: Bad certhash (spaces/non-hex), wrong appid, cert not in LocalMachine store, or port already bound.
items.diagnosticCommand: netsh http show sslcert ; verify thumbprint with no spaces in LocalMachine\My.
items.knownFix: Delete stale binding then add with clean certhash and valid appid GUID.
items.preventionRule: Copy thumbprint programmatically and strip whitespace.
items.detectBy: netsh sslcert error code 1312/1168 in deploy logs.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: clean thumbprint and confirm cert store before retry.

items.title: appsettings / environment configuration mistakes
items.description: Symptoms: app reads the wrong connection string, a feature flag is off in production, or settings 'work locally but not deployed'. Root cause: ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT differs between machines so appsettings.{Environment}.json overrides change, environment variables override file values unexpectedly, or a transformed config was not deployed. Diagnostic: log the effective configuration source for key settings at startup and confirm ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT. Known fix: align the environment variable, ensure the correct appsettings.{Environment}.json is deployed, and understand the configuration provider order (later providers win). Prevention rule: keep secrets out of files and use environment variables for per-host values; never commit credentials. LocalAIFactory detection: effective config differs from expected. Fix/warn: warn to check ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT and provider precedence before debugging code.
items.applicability: Any ASP.NET Core app using layered configuration across environments.
items.example: Prod used a stale connection string because ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT was unset and the Development file loaded.
items.limitation: Provider order can be customized in Program.cs; verify the actual builder order.
items.symptoms: Wrong connection string/flag in deployed env; 'works locally'.
items.rootCause: Environment mismatch, provider override precedence, or untransformed config deployed.
items.diagnosticCommand: Log effective config values + ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT at startup.
items.knownFix: Align environment variable, deploy correct appsettings.{Environment}.json, respect provider order.
items.preventionRule: Keep secrets in environment variables, never in committed files.
items.detectBy: Effective config differs from expected at startup.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: check ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT and provider precedence first.

items.title: stdout logging / locked log file issues
items.description: Symptoms: no diagnostic logs despite stdout logging 'enabled', the app pool recycles, or an access-denied/locked-file error on the log path. Root cause: the logs folder does not exist or the app pool identity cannot write to it, stdoutLogEnabled is left true in production (causing growth and contention), or a previous process still holds the file. Diagnostic: temporarily set stdoutLogEnabled=true with a writable stdoutLogFile path under the app folder, reproduce, then read the captured startup error. Known fix: create the log directory, grant write to the app pool identity, capture the error, then disable stdout logging again. Prevention rule: use stdout logging only briefly for startup diagnosis; rely on structured logging otherwise. LocalAIFactory detection: empty/locked log path during a failed start. Fix/warn: warn to enable stdout logging temporarily to capture the real startup exception.
items.applicability: Diagnosing ASP.NET Core startup failures under IIS/ANCM.
items.example: 500.30 with no logs; enabling stdout logging to a writable path reveals a config exception.
items.limitation: Leaving stdout logging on permanently degrades performance and fills disk.
items.symptoms: No logs, recycles, or locked/access-denied on the log file.
items.rootCause: Missing/unwritable log folder, stdout logging left on, or file held by a prior process.
items.diagnosticCommand: Set stdoutLogEnabled=true with a writable stdoutLogFile, reproduce, read captured output.
items.knownFix: Create log dir, grant write to app-pool identity, capture error, then disable stdout logging.
items.preventionRule: Use stdout logging only briefly for startup diagnosis.
items.detectBy: Empty or locked stdout log path during a failed start.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: enable stdout logging temporarily to capture the startup exception.

items.title: IIS 500.19 (configuration data invalid)
items.description: Symptoms: HTTP 500.19 'cannot read configuration file' or 'invalid configuration data', often with an error code like 0x8007000d. Root cause: a malformed web.config (XML error, duplicated section), a referenced IIS module/feature not installed (e.g. URL Rewrite, the aspNetCore handler), or NTFS permissions blocking the config read. Diagnostic: validate web.config as XML and check the precise sub-status/error code; confirm required IIS features/modules are installed. Known fix: correct the web.config XML, install the missing module (URL Rewrite / Hosting Bundle), and fix folder permissions. Prevention rule: validate web.config in the publish step and ensure required modules ship with the host. LocalAIFactory detection: 500.19 with a config error code. Fix/warn: warn to validate web.config and install the missing IIS module before code changes.
items.applicability: IIS sites whose web.config references modules not present on the host.
items.example: 500.19 0x8007000d because URL Rewrite was referenced but not installed; installing the module fixes it.
items.limitation: Some 500.19 causes are deep NTFS ACL issues that need per-folder inspection.
items.symptoms: HTTP 500.19 'invalid configuration data' / cannot read config.
items.rootCause: Malformed web.config, missing IIS module, or NTFS permission on config.
items.diagnosticCommand: Validate web.config XML; check the 500.19 sub-status/error code; confirm required IIS modules installed.
items.knownFix: Fix web.config XML, install missing module (URL Rewrite/Hosting Bundle), correct permissions.
items.preventionRule: Validate web.config in publish; ship required modules with the host.
items.detectBy: 500.19 with a configuration error code.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: validate web.config and install missing module first.

items.title: ASP.NET Core 500.30 in-process startup failure
items.description: Symptoms: HTTP 500.30 'ASP.NET Core app failed to start'. Root cause: an unhandled exception during host build/startup — a bad connection string, a DI registration error, a missing dependency, or a configuration binding failure. The 500.30 itself only says the process started then crashed; the real cause is in the startup exception. Diagnostic: enable stdout logging or read the Windows Application event log for the ASP.NET Core Module event containing the exception; alternatively run 'dotnet App.dll' from the publish folder to see the stack trace directly. Known fix: address the specific startup exception (config, DI, dependency). Prevention rule: fail fast with clear startup validation and test the publish output via Kestrel before IIS. LocalAIFactory detection: 500.30 response. Fix/warn: per the KB rule, check Event Viewer + stdout logs + appsettings + runtime bundle before changing code.
items.applicability: In-process hosted ASP.NET Core apps under IIS/ANCM.
items.example: 500.30 caused by a missing connection string; the event log shows the exact configuration exception.
items.limitation: 500.30 is a symptom umbrella; the fix depends entirely on the captured exception.
items.symptoms: HTTP 500.30 'app failed to start'.
items.rootCause: Unhandled startup exception: config/DI/dependency/binding.
items.diagnosticCommand: Run 'dotnet ./publish/App.dll' to see the startup stack trace; or read the ANCM event-log entry.
items.knownFix: Fix the specific captured startup exception.
items.preventionRule: Validate startup fast and test publish via Kestrel before IIS.
items.detectBy: 500.30 response from the site.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: check Event Viewer + stdout logs + appsettings + runtime bundle before code changes.

items.title: ASP.NET Core 502.5 process startup failure
items.description: Symptoms: HTTP 502.5 'process failure' (typical of out-of-process hosting) — IIS could not start or could not connect to the backend dotnet process. Root cause: the wrong dotnet runtime/processPath in web.config, the process crashed immediately, or a port/handshake timeout between ANCM and Kestrel. Diagnostic: confirm 'dotnet --list-runtimes' includes the target framework, run the app directly with 'dotnet App.dll' from the publish folder, and check the ANCM event log. Known fix: install/repair the runtime, correct processPath/arguments in web.config, and resolve whatever crash the direct run reveals. Prevention rule: pin the runtime and validate the publish runs standalone. LocalAIFactory detection: 502.5 response. Fix/warn: warn that ANCM could not start the worker — verify runtime + processPath + a clean direct run before code changes.
items.applicability: Out-of-process (or misconfigured in-process) ASP.NET Core hosting under IIS.
items.example: 502.5 because the target runtime was not installed; 'dotnet --list-runtimes' confirms the gap.
items.limitation: In-process failures usually surface as 500.30 instead; check the exact sub-status.
items.symptoms: HTTP 502.5 process failure; ANCM cannot start/reach the worker.
items.rootCause: Missing runtime, wrong processPath, immediate crash, or ANCM/Kestrel handshake timeout.
items.diagnosticCommand: dotnet --list-runtimes ; run 'dotnet ./publish/App.dll' ; read ANCM event log.
items.knownFix: Install/repair runtime, fix processPath/arguments, resolve the revealed crash.
items.preventionRule: Pin runtime; verify the publish runs standalone before IIS.
items.detectBy: 502.5 response from the site.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: verify runtime + processPath + clean direct run before code changes.

items.title: LocalDB vs SQL Express behavioral differences
items.description: Symptoms: app works against (localdb)\MSSQLLocalDB in development but fails when pointed at .\SQLEXPRESS in deployment, or vice versa. Root cause: LocalDB is a user-instance, on-demand engine with no network endpoint and a per-user lifecycle, while SQL Express is a always-running service reachable over TCP with instance-level auth and permissions. Connection strings, auth model (current user vs app-pool identity), and service availability all differ. Diagnostic: confirm the target instance type and use sqlcmd against each to compare. Known fix: standardize on SQL Express for server deployments, set the correct connection string, and provision the app-pool SQL login. Prevention rule: develop and test against the same engine you deploy to. LocalAIFactory detection: connection string names localdb while deploying to a server. Fix/warn: warn that LocalDB is not a server engine; switch to SQL Express with proper login.
items.applicability: Teams that develop on LocalDB but deploy on SQL Express / full SQL Server.
items.example: A localdb connection string fails under IIS because the app-pool identity cannot reach a per-user instance.
items.limitation: Some features differ across editions beyond connectivity; verify edition capabilities.
items.symptoms: Works on LocalDB in dev, fails on SQL Express in deployment (or reverse).
items.rootCause: LocalDB is per-user on-demand with no network endpoint; SQL Express is a networked service.
items.diagnosticCommand: sqlcmd against each instance to confirm type and reachability.
items.knownFix: Standardize on SQL Express for servers; fix connection string; provision app-pool login.
items.preventionRule: Develop and test on the same engine you deploy to.
items.detectBy: Connection string names localdb while deploying to a server.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: LocalDB is not a server engine; switch to SQL Express with proper login.

items.title: Database backup / restore failure
items.description: Symptoms: BACKUP/RESTORE fails with 'operating system error 5 (access denied)', 'exclusive access could not be obtained', or 'the backup set holds a backup of a database other than the existing'. Root cause: the SQL service account cannot read/write the backup path, active connections hold the database during restore, or RESTORE was run without WITH REPLACE / correct MOVE for relocated files. Diagnostic: verify the SQL service account has NTFS rights to the backup folder; set the database to SINGLE_USER WITH ROLLBACK IMMEDIATE before restore; inspect RESTORE HEADERONLY/FILELISTONLY. Known fix: grant the service account folder rights, kill/redirect connections, and use WITH REPLACE/MOVE as needed. Prevention rule: store backups on a path the SQL service can access and script restore with explicit MOVE. LocalAIFactory detection: backup/restore job error codes. Fix/warn: warn to check SQL service-account path rights and active connections before retrying.
items.applicability: Operational backup/restore runbooks for SQL Server/Express.
items.example: Restore fails with OS error 5 because the SQL service account lacks rights to the backup share.
items.limitation: Point-in-time/log-chain restore has additional sequencing requirements not covered here.
items.symptoms: BACKUP/RESTORE access-denied, exclusive-access, or wrong-backup-set errors.
items.rootCause: SQL service-account path rights, active connections, or missing WITH REPLACE/MOVE.
items.diagnosticCommand: RESTORE FILELISTONLY/HEADERONLY ; verify SQL service account NTFS rights; SET SINGLE_USER WITH ROLLBACK IMMEDIATE.
items.knownFix: Grant folder rights to SQL service account, clear connections, use WITH REPLACE/MOVE.
items.preventionRule: Store backups where the SQL service can read/write; script restore with explicit MOVE.
items.detectBy: Backup/restore job error code (5 / exclusive access / wrong backup set).
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: check service-account path rights and active connections first.

items.title: File / folder permission failure (NTFS access denied)
items.description: Symptoms: UnauthorizedAccessException, 'access to the path is denied', or write failures to a data/keys/log directory under the app-pool identity. Root cause: the app-pool/service identity lacks NTFS Modify rights on the target folder, inheritance is broken, or the path is under a protected location (Program Files) that blocks writes. Diagnostic: icacls <path> to read the ACL and confirm the running identity; check the exact path in the exception. Known fix: grant Modify to the app-pool identity on the specific data folder, or relocate writable data out of protected paths. Prevention rule: keep writable data (keys/, logs, uploads) in a dedicated folder with explicit ACLs set at deploy time. LocalAIFactory detection: UnauthorizedAccessException on a known data path (e.g. keys/ for Data Protection). Fix/warn: warn to grant least-privilege Modify on that path, not broad permissions.
items.applicability: Apps that write keys, logs, uploads, or caches under a restricted Windows identity.
items.example: Data Protection cannot write to keys/ because the app-pool identity lacks Modify on that folder.
items.limitation: Granting broad rights to fix this is itself a risk; keep it scoped.
items.symptoms: UnauthorizedAccessException / access denied writing to a data/keys/log path.
items.rootCause: App-pool identity lacks NTFS Modify, broken inheritance, or protected path.
items.diagnosticCommand: icacls <path> ; read the exact denied path from the exception.
items.knownFix: Grant Modify to the app-pool identity on the specific folder, or relocate writable data.
items.preventionRule: Keep writable data in a dedicated folder with explicit ACLs at deploy time.
items.detectBy: UnauthorizedAccessException on a known writable path (e.g. keys/).
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: grant scoped Modify on that path, not broad permissions.

items.title: Windows service / process file-lock failure
items.description: Symptoms: deploy/copy fails with 'the process cannot access the file because it is being used by another process', or a DLL cannot be overwritten during publish. Root cause: a running app-pool worker, a Windows service, or a lingering dotnet process still holds the assembly/file. Diagnostic: identify the holding process (e.g. via handle/resource monitor) or stop the IIS site/app pool before copying. Known fix: stop the app pool/site (or service), deploy, then restart; use an app_offline.htm to drain the app before file replacement. Prevention rule: place app_offline.htm before copying files and remove it after. LocalAIFactory detection: file-in-use error during a deploy step. Fix/warn: warn to stop the site/app pool (or drop app_offline.htm) before replacing locked files.
items.applicability: In-place ASP.NET Core deployments where files are overwritten while the app runs.
items.example: Publish fails to overwrite App.dll because the IIS worker holds it; app_offline.htm releases it.
items.limitation: Some locks are held by antivirus/indexing rather than the app; check those too.
items.symptoms: 'File in use' / cannot overwrite assembly during deploy.
items.rootCause: Running worker/service/dotnet process holds the file.
items.diagnosticCommand: Identify holding process; stop the IIS site/app pool or drop app_offline.htm.
items.knownFix: Stop app pool/site (or use app_offline.htm), deploy, restart.
items.preventionRule: Use app_offline.htm before copying and remove it after.
items.detectBy: File-in-use error during a deploy/copy step.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: stop the site/app pool or drop app_offline.htm before replacing locked files.

items.title: Playwright / headless browser install failure
items.description: Symptoms: Playwright tests fail with 'browser executable doesn't exist' or 'Host system is missing dependencies', often after CI/host changes. Root cause: the browser binaries were never installed (playwright install), the install ran for the wrong Node/.NET runtime, or Linux OS library dependencies are missing. Diagnostic: run the framework's install command (e.g. pwsh bin/.../playwright.ps1 install for .NET, or npx playwright install) and read which browser/dependency is missing. Known fix: install the browsers and OS dependencies for the host; pin the Playwright version to match the driver. Prevention rule: make browser install an explicit, version-pinned step in setup. LocalAIFactory detection: 'browser executable doesn't exist'. Fix/warn: warn to run the Playwright install step for the host before treating it as a test bug.
items.applicability: Browser-driven UI verification and benchmark harnesses using Playwright.
items.example: Tests fail on a fresh runner because 'playwright install' was never executed for the host.
items.limitation: Air-gapped hosts need pre-downloaded browser bundles; install-from-internet will fail.
items.symptoms: 'browser executable doesn't exist' / missing host dependencies.
items.rootCause: Browsers not installed, wrong runtime, or missing OS libraries.
items.diagnosticCommand: Run the Playwright install command and read the missing browser/dependency.
items.knownFix: Install browsers + OS dependencies; pin Playwright version to driver.
items.preventionRule: Make browser install an explicit version-pinned setup step.
items.detectBy: 'browser executable doesn't exist' at test start.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: run the Playwright install step for the host before debugging tests.

items.title: GitHub release asset upload failure
items.description: Symptoms: gh release upload fails with 'asset already exists', a 401/403, or an upload timeout on large assets. Root cause: an asset of the same name already exists on the release, the token lacks the required scope/permissions, or the asset exceeds size/timeout limits. Diagnostic: gh release view <tag> to list existing assets and confirm token scopes. Known fix: use --clobber to overwrite an existing asset, grant the token the needed repo scope, and split or retry large uploads. Prevention rule: generate unique versioned asset names and verify token scope before release. LocalAIFactory detection: 'asset already exists' or 401/403 from gh. Fix/warn: warn to clobber/rename the asset and check token scope before retrying.
items.applicability: Release automation that uploads packaged artifacts to GitHub Releases.
items.example: Upload fails 'asset already exists'; re-running with --clobber overwrites it.
items.limitation: Network/proxy limits in restricted environments may still block uploads.
items.symptoms: 'asset already exists', 401/403, or large-asset timeout from gh release upload.
items.rootCause: Duplicate asset name, insufficient token scope, or size/timeout limit.
items.diagnosticCommand: gh release view <tag> ; verify token scopes.
items.knownFix: Use --clobber, grant repo scope, retry/split large uploads.
items.preventionRule: Use unique versioned asset names; verify token scope pre-release.
items.detectBy: 'asset already exists' or 401/403 from gh.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: clobber/rename and check token scope before retry.

items.title: PowerShell quoting / path escaping issues
items.description: Symptoms: a script command fails with 'a positional parameter cannot be found', mangles a path with spaces, or a native exe receives wrong arguments. Root cause: PowerShell parses arguments differently from cmd/bash — unquoted paths with spaces split, arguments starting with - are treated as parameters, and variable expansion inside double quotes alters intended literals. Diagnostic: echo the constructed argument array, use the call operator & with quoted paths, and the stop-parsing token --% for literal native args. Known fix: quote paths with spaces, use single quotes for literals, and & 'C:\Path With Spaces\app.exe' for invocation. Prevention rule: build argument lists as arrays and avoid string concatenation for command lines. LocalAIFactory detection: PowerShell parameter/positional errors in deploy scripts. Fix/warn: warn to quote paths and use the call operator / --% before assuming a tool bug.
items.applicability: PowerShell deployment and automation scripts invoking native executables.
items.example: appcmd call fails because a path with spaces split into two arguments; quoting fixes it.
items.limitation: Cross-shell scripts (bash vs PowerShell) need shell-specific quoting; one form is not portable.
items.symptoms: Positional-parameter error, split path, or wrong native args.
items.rootCause: PowerShell argument parsing: unquoted spaces, leading-dash parsing, double-quote expansion.
items.diagnosticCommand: Echo the argument array; invoke with & and quoted paths; use --% for literal native args.
items.knownFix: Quote paths, use single quotes for literals, invoke via the call operator.
items.preventionRule: Build argument lists as arrays, not concatenated strings.
items.detectBy: PowerShell parameter/positional errors in a deploy script.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: quote paths and use the call operator / --% before blaming the tool.

items.title: Long-path / clone-timeout issues (and clone-retry 'destination exists')
items.description: Symptoms: git clone fails with 'Filename too long', times out on a large repo, and a naive retry then fails with 'destination path already exists and is not an empty directory'. Root cause: Windows MAX_PATH limits without core.longpaths, slow/large repos exceeding a fixed timeout, and a partial clone leaving a non-empty target directory that blocks retry. Firsthand finding in this project: the clone-retry path failed because the partial clone directory was not removed before retrying. Diagnostic: check git config core.longpaths, the depth of nested paths, and whether the target dir already exists. Known fix: set core.longpaths=true, use a shallow clone (--depth) or longer timeout, and DELETE the partial clone directory before retrying. Prevention rule: clean the destination directory on the failure path before any retry. LocalAIFactory detection: clone timeout followed by 'destination exists'. Fix/warn: warn to delete the partial clone dir before retrying and enable long paths.
items.applicability: Repo import/benchmark harnesses cloning large or deeply-nested repositories on Windows.
items.example: A timed-out clone left a partial folder; the retry failed until the partial dir was deleted first.
items.limitation: Some filesystems/tools still cap paths even with longpaths enabled.
items.symptoms: 'Filename too long', clone timeout, then 'destination path already exists' on retry.
items.rootCause: MAX_PATH limit, large-repo timeout, and leftover partial clone directory.
items.diagnosticCommand: git config --get core.longpaths ; test if the target clone dir exists/non-empty.
items.knownFix: Enable core.longpaths, shallow-clone or extend timeout, and delete the partial dir before retry.
items.preventionRule: On the failure path, clean the destination directory before retrying.
items.detectBy: Clone timeout followed by 'destination exists' on retry.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: delete the partial clone dir before retry and enable long paths.

items.title: Large-repo benchmark failure (resource/time exhaustion)
items.description: Symptoms: an import/analysis benchmark on a very large repository hangs, runs out of memory, or exceeds a step timeout. Root cause: unbounded file enumeration, materializing entire files into memory, or a single transaction over too many rows. Diagnostic: measure peak memory and per-step time, identify the file count and largest files, and check for unbounded loads. Known fix: stream/batch file processing, cap per-file size, paginate database writes, and raise or split the step timeout. Prevention rule: enforce per-repo limits (max files, max file size) and process in bounded batches. LocalAIFactory detection: benchmark exceeds time/memory thresholds on large repos. Fix/warn: warn to batch and bound resource usage rather than increasing limits blindly.
items.applicability: Repo-import and code-analysis benchmarks over real-world large codebases.
items.example: A benchmark OOMs on a monorepo because all files were read into memory at once; batching fixes it.
items.limitation: Some repos are simply too large for a single host; needs partitioning.
items.symptoms: Benchmark hang, OOM, or step-timeout on a large repo.
items.rootCause: Unbounded enumeration, full-file materialization, or oversized single transactions.
items.diagnosticCommand: Measure peak memory and per-step time; count files and largest file sizes.
items.knownFix: Stream/batch processing, cap file size, paginate writes, split timeouts.
items.preventionRule: Enforce per-repo limits and process in bounded batches.
items.detectBy: Benchmark exceeds time/memory thresholds on large repos.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: batch and bound usage rather than raising limits blindly.

items.title: Ollama model-availability issue (optional inference absent)
items.description: Symptoms: chat/inference features return errors like 'model not found' or connection refused, or the feature should degrade but instead throws. Root cause: Ollama is not running, the configured model was never pulled, or the app treats an optional dependency as mandatory. Diagnostic: curl http://localhost:11434/api/tags to list available models and confirm the service is up. Known fix: pull the configured model (ollama pull <model>) or, per project rules, ensure the feature degrades gracefully when Ollama is absent (MSSQL-only mode must still work). Prevention rule: gate Ollama behind a config flag and never make a page depend on it. LocalAIFactory detection: inference call fails or model missing. Fix/warn: warn that Ollama is optional — pull the model or fall back; never block a page on it.
items.applicability: Optional local-inference features in an MSSQL-only-capable deployment.
items.example: Chat errors 'model not found' because nomic-embed-text/qwen2.5-coder was not pulled; pulling it resolves it.
items.limitation: Air-gapped hosts cannot pull models on demand; pre-stage them.
items.symptoms: 'model not found' / connection refused, or a hard failure where degrade was expected.
items.rootCause: Ollama down, model not pulled, or optional dependency treated as mandatory.
items.diagnosticCommand: curl http://localhost:11434/api/tags
items.knownFix: Pull the configured model, or ensure graceful degradation when Ollama is absent.
items.preventionRule: Gate Ollama behind a config flag; never make a page depend on it.
items.detectBy: Inference call fails or returns 'model not found'.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: Ollama is optional — pull the model or fall back; never block a page.

items.title: Qdrant / vector-store connection issue (optional RAG absent)
items.description: Symptoms: retrieval features error or a page stalls because a Qdrant call is on the request path. Root cause: Qdrant is not running, the REST endpoint/port is wrong, or a synchronous Qdrant probe was placed in a controller/view (forbidden by project rules). Diagnostic: curl http://localhost:6333/collections to confirm reachability; check that health is read from the cached snapshot, not a live call. Known fix: read Qdrant health from IServiceHealthCache, make retrieval degrade gracefully when Qdrant is absent, and remove any synchronous probe from the request path. Prevention rule: Qdrant is optional and REST-only; never call it synchronously inside a controller action or Razor view. LocalAIFactory detection: a page stalls and a Qdrant call sits on the request path, or retrieval errors. Fix/warn: warn to move the probe off the request path and degrade gracefully.
items.applicability: Optional RAG/vector retrieval in an MSSQL-only-capable deployment.
items.example: A page hangs because a controller probed Qdrant synchronously; reading the cached health snapshot fixes the hang.
items.limitation: Without Qdrant, semantic retrieval is unavailable; keyword/SQL paths must cover the gap.
items.symptoms: Retrieval errors or a page stall caused by a live Qdrant call.
items.rootCause: Qdrant down/misconfigured, or a synchronous probe on the request path.
items.diagnosticCommand: curl http://localhost:6333/collections ; confirm health comes from the cache, not a live call.
items.knownFix: Read Qdrant health from IServiceHealthCache; degrade retrieval gracefully; remove synchronous probes.
items.preventionRule: Qdrant is optional and REST-only; never call it synchronously in a controller or view.
items.detectBy: Page stall with a Qdrant call on the request path, or retrieval errors.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: move the probe off the request path and degrade gracefully.

items.title: OCR / PDF processing failure
items.description: Symptoms: OCR returns empty/garbled text, a PDF cannot be parsed, or the engine (e.g. Tesseract) is not found. Root cause: a scanned/image-only PDF with no text layer requires OCR not text extraction, missing OCR engine/language data, low DPI/poor image quality, or an encrypted/corrupt PDF. Diagnostic: check whether the PDF has a text layer vs is image-only; verify the OCR engine and language data are installed; inspect DPI. Known fix: route image-only PDFs through OCR with adequate DPI and the correct language pack; extract text directly when a text layer exists; handle encrypted PDFs explicitly. Prevention rule: detect text-layer presence and branch accordingly; retain provenance (extracted vs OCR'd). LocalAIFactory detection: empty/garbled OCR output or parse failure. Fix/warn: warn to verify text-layer/engine/DPI and keep provenance; never assert OCR text as authoritative without confidence.
items.applicability: Document-intelligence pipelines processing mixed scanned and digital PDFs.
items.example: An image-only cheque scan yields empty text via extraction; routing it through OCR at higher DPI recovers it.
items.limitation: OCR accuracy is dataset/pipeline dependent; no fixed accuracy is implied and high-risk reads need human review.
items.symptoms: Empty/garbled OCR text, PDF parse failure, or OCR engine not found.
items.rootCause: Image-only PDF, missing engine/language data, low DPI, or encrypted/corrupt PDF.
items.diagnosticCommand: Detect text-layer presence; verify OCR engine + language data; inspect DPI.
items.knownFix: Route image-only PDFs through OCR at adequate DPI with correct language; extract directly when text exists.
items.preventionRule: Branch on text-layer presence; retain provenance (extracted vs OCR).
items.detectBy: Empty/garbled OCR output or PDF parse failure.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: verify text-layer/engine/DPI; keep provenance; never assert OCR text as authoritative.

items.title: SFTP / SMTP integration failure
items.description: Symptoms: SFTP transfers fail with host-key/auth errors; SMTP send fails with auth, TLS, or relay-denied errors. Root cause (SFTP): unknown/changed host key, wrong key/password auth, or firewall on port 22. Root cause (SMTP): wrong port/TLS mode (465 implicit vs 587 STARTTLS), failed auth, or the relay rejecting the sender. Diagnostic: test SFTP with a known-good client and verify the host key fingerprint; test SMTP connectivity to the port and TLS mode and read the server reply code. Known fix: pin the SFTP host key and correct credentials; set the right SMTP port/TLS and authenticate, ensuring the sender is permitted to relay. Prevention rule: store host keys and credentials securely (no secrets in repo) and validate connectivity in a health check. LocalAIFactory detection: integration job fails with auth/TLS/relay error. Fix/warn: warn to verify host key / port / TLS mode / credentials before code changes.
items.applicability: Outbound file transfer and email notification integrations.
items.example: SMTP send fails because port 587 needed STARTTLS but implicit TLS was configured; switching modes fixes it.
items.limitation: Provider-specific relay policies vary; sender authorization may need DNS (SPF/DKIM) setup.
items.symptoms: SFTP host-key/auth failure; SMTP auth/TLS/relay-denied error.
items.rootCause: SFTP: host key/credentials/firewall. SMTP: port/TLS mode/auth/relay policy.
items.diagnosticCommand: Test SFTP host-key fingerprint with a known client; test SMTP port+TLS and read the reply code.
items.knownFix: Pin host key + correct creds; set correct SMTP port/TLS and authenticate as a permitted sender.
items.preventionRule: Store host keys/credentials securely (no secrets in repo); validate in a health check.
items.detectBy: Integration job fails with auth/TLS/relay error.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: verify host key / port / TLS / credentials before code changes.

items.title: WordPress REST API integration failure
items.description: Symptoms: WordPress REST calls return 401/403, a rest_no_route 404, or the Authorization header is missing on the server. Root cause: application passwords not used (or basic auth stripped by Apache so the header never arrives), permalinks set to plain (breaking /wp-json/ routes), or insufficient user capabilities. Diagnostic: GET /wp-json/ to confirm the API base resolves; verify the Authorization header reaches PHP and the user has the needed capability. Known fix: use Application Passwords, ensure the server forwards the Authorization header, set pretty permalinks, and grant the right role. Prevention rule: store the application password as a secret and confirm header forwarding in the target host. LocalAIFactory detection: 401/403/rest_no_route from a WordPress integration. Fix/warn: warn to check application password, header forwarding, permalinks, and capabilities first.
items.applicability: Integrations reading/writing WordPress content via the REST API.
items.example: 401 because Apache stripped the Authorization header; enabling header pass-through fixes auth.
items.limitation: Plugin/security hardening can further restrict the REST API beyond core behavior.
items.symptoms: 401/403, rest_no_route 404, or missing Authorization header.
items.rootCause: No application password, stripped auth header, plain permalinks, or insufficient capability.
items.diagnosticCommand: GET /wp-json/ to confirm the base; verify the Authorization header reaches PHP.
items.knownFix: Use Application Passwords, forward the auth header, set pretty permalinks, grant the right role.
items.preventionRule: Store the application password securely; confirm header forwarding on the host.
items.detectBy: 401/403/rest_no_route from a WordPress call.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: check app password, header forwarding, permalinks, capabilities first.

items.title: Odoo external API (JSON-RPC / XML-RPC) integration failure
items.description: Symptoms: Odoo RPC calls fail to authenticate, return access-rights errors, or 'database not found'. Root cause: wrong database name on a multi-DB server, an API key required instead of a password (newer Odoo), or the user lacks model access rights / record rules block the operation. Diagnostic: call the common/authenticate endpoint to get a uid, confirm the database name, and test a simple read on an allowed model. Known fix: use the correct database and an API key (or valid credentials), and ensure the integration user has the required access rights and groups. Prevention rule: provision a dedicated integration user with least-privilege model access and store the API key as a secret. LocalAIFactory detection: Odoo auth/access-rights/db-not-found error. Fix/warn: warn to verify database name, API key/credentials, and user access rights first.
items.applicability: ERP integrations reading/writing Odoo models over RPC.
items.example: 'Access Denied' because the integration user lacked the model's access rights; granting the group fixes it.
items.limitation: Record rules can still restrict specific records even with model access.
items.symptoms: Auth failure, access-rights error, or 'database not found' from Odoo RPC.
items.rootCause: Wrong database, missing API key/credentials, or insufficient model access/record rules.
items.diagnosticCommand: Call common/authenticate for a uid; confirm DB name; test a read on an allowed model.
items.knownFix: Use correct DB + API key/credentials; grant the integration user required access rights/groups.
items.preventionRule: Dedicated least-privilege integration user; store API key as a secret.
items.detectBy: Odoo auth/access-rights/db-not-found error.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: verify DB name, API key/credentials, and access rights first.

items.title: ERPNext / Frappe REST API integration failure
items.description: Symptoms: ERPNext/Frappe API returns 401/403, 'PermissionError', or CSRF/token errors. Root cause: missing token-based auth (api_key:api_secret in the Authorization: token header), insufficient role permissions on the DocType, or attempting a stateful call without the right auth mode. Diagnostic: GET /api/method/frappe.auth.get_logged_user with the token header to confirm auth, then test a DocType read. Known fix: use 'Authorization: token api_key:api_secret', grant the integration user the needed DocType role/permissions, and use the correct endpoint form. Prevention rule: create a dedicated API user with scoped roles and store the api_secret as a secret. LocalAIFactory detection: 401/403/PermissionError from Frappe. Fix/warn: warn to verify the token header format and DocType permissions first.
items.applicability: ERP integrations against ERPNext / Frappe framework apps.
items.example: 401 because the token header used basic-auth format; switching to 'token key:secret' authenticates.
items.limitation: Frappe permission rules (user permissions, conditional perms) can still block records.
items.symptoms: 401/403, PermissionError, or CSRF/token error from Frappe.
items.rootCause: Wrong/missing token header, insufficient DocType role, or wrong auth mode.
items.diagnosticCommand: GET /api/method/frappe.auth.get_logged_user with the token header.
items.knownFix: Use 'Authorization: token api_key:api_secret', grant DocType roles, use correct endpoints.
items.preventionRule: Dedicated scoped API user; store api_secret as a secret.
items.detectBy: 401/403/PermissionError from Frappe.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: verify token header format and DocType permissions first.

items.title: WooCommerce REST API integration failure
items.description: Symptoms: WooCommerce REST returns 401 'consumer key is missing', signature errors, or works over HTTPS but fails over HTTP. Root cause: consumer key/secret not sent (or sent as query params over HTTPS where header basic-auth is expected), OAuth1 signature required for HTTP endpoints, or a server stripping the Authorization header. Diagnostic: GET /wp-json/wc/v3/ with the consumer key/secret as basic auth over HTTPS; confirm the header reaches the server. Known fix: use basic auth (key as username, secret as password) over HTTPS, or OAuth1.0a signing over HTTP, and ensure header forwarding. Prevention rule: always use HTTPS with basic auth and store keys as secrets. LocalAIFactory detection: 401/consumer-key/signature errors from WooCommerce. Fix/warn: warn to use HTTPS+basic auth and verify header forwarding before debugging.
items.applicability: E-commerce integrations against WooCommerce stores.
items.example: 401 over HTTP because OAuth1 signing was required; switching to HTTPS basic auth fixes it.
items.limitation: Some hosts and security plugins still strip auth headers regardless.
items.symptoms: 401 'consumer key missing', signature error, or HTTP-only auth failure.
items.rootCause: Keys not sent correctly, OAuth1 signature needed over HTTP, or stripped auth header.
items.diagnosticCommand: GET /wp-json/wc/v3/ with key/secret as basic auth over HTTPS.
items.knownFix: Use HTTPS basic auth (key:secret) or OAuth1 over HTTP; ensure header forwarding.
items.preventionRule: Use HTTPS + basic auth; store keys as secrets.
items.detectBy: 401/consumer-key/signature error from WooCommerce.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: use HTTPS+basic auth and verify header forwarding first.

items.title: Magento REST/GraphQL API integration failure
items.description: Symptoms: Magento API returns 401, a token-expired error, or 'consumer is not authorized to access resource'. Root cause: an expired/short-lived admin token, missing integration access-control resources, or wrong base path (rest/V1 vs a store-scoped path). Diagnostic: POST to integration/admin/token to obtain a bearer token, then call rest/V1/... with it; check the integration's resource ACL. Known fix: refresh the token, grant the integration the required ACL resources, and use the correct (store-scoped) endpoint. Prevention rule: use an integration with scoped ACLs and handle token refresh. LocalAIFactory detection: 401/token-expired/not-authorized from Magento. Fix/warn: warn to refresh the token and verify the integration ACL/endpoint first.
items.applicability: E-commerce integrations against Magento (Adobe Commerce) stores.
items.example: 'consumer is not authorized' because the integration lacked the resource ACL; granting it fixes the call.
items.limitation: Token TTL and ACL granularity vary by Magento version/config.
items.symptoms: 401, token expired, or 'not authorized to access resource' from Magento.
items.rootCause: Expired token, missing ACL resources, or wrong/store-scoped endpoint.
items.diagnosticCommand: POST integration/admin/token for a bearer token; call rest/V1/... ; review the integration ACL.
items.knownFix: Refresh token, grant required ACL resources, use correct endpoint.
items.preventionRule: Use a scoped integration and handle token refresh.
items.detectBy: 401/token-expired/not-authorized from Magento.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: refresh token and verify ACL/endpoint first.

items.title: Drupal JSON:API / REST integration failure
items.description: Symptoms: Drupal JSON:API returns 403/406, '406 Not Acceptable', or a missing-module 404. Root cause: the JSON:API/REST module not enabled, a missing or incorrect Accept header (application/vnd.api+json), or permissions/roles denying the operation. Diagnostic: confirm the JSON:API module is enabled and GET /jsonapi with the correct Accept header; check the user's permissions. Known fix: enable the module, send the required Accept/Content-Type headers, and grant the role the needed permission. Prevention rule: pin the API module set and use a dedicated role with scoped permissions. LocalAIFactory detection: 403/406/404 from Drupal API. Fix/warn: warn to verify module enablement, headers, and permissions first.
items.applicability: CMS integrations against Drupal sites exposing JSON:API/REST.
items.example: 406 because the Accept header was application/json instead of application/vnd.api+json; correcting it fixes the call.
items.limitation: Drupal access-control and field-level permissions can further restrict responses.
items.symptoms: 403/406 or missing-module 404 from Drupal API.
items.rootCause: JSON:API/REST module disabled, wrong Accept header, or insufficient permissions.
items.diagnosticCommand: GET /jsonapi with Accept: application/vnd.api+json ; confirm the module is enabled.
items.knownFix: Enable the module, send correct headers, grant the role permissions.
items.preventionRule: Pin the API module set; use a scoped role.
items.detectBy: 403/406/404 from Drupal API.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: verify module, headers, and permissions first.

items.title: Keycloak / OIDC integration failure
items.description: Symptoms: OIDC login fails with invalid redirect_uri, signature/issuer validation errors, or a token the app rejects. Root cause: redirect URI not registered exactly (scheme/host/port/path), clock skew, a wrong issuer/authority or audience, or the app not trusting the IdP's signing keys (JWKS). Diagnostic: fetch the .well-known/openid-configuration to confirm issuer/JWKS endpoints; compare the registered redirect URI to the actual one; check token aud/iss claims. Known fix: register the exact redirect URI, align issuer/authority and audience, allow small clock skew, and ensure JWKS is reachable. Prevention rule: configure issuer/audience/redirect from a single source of truth and trust JWKS by discovery. LocalAIFactory detection: OIDC redirect/signature/issuer errors. Fix/warn: warn to verify redirect URI, issuer/audience, and JWKS before code changes.
items.applicability: SSO integrations using Keycloak or any OIDC-compliant identity provider.
items.example: invalid redirect_uri because the registered path omitted the trailing segment; exact registration fixes login.
items.limitation: Reverse proxies can rewrite the redirect host; see the forwarded-headers issue.
items.symptoms: invalid redirect_uri, signature/issuer validation error, or rejected token.
items.rootCause: Unregistered redirect URI, clock skew, wrong issuer/audience, or untrusted JWKS.
items.diagnosticCommand: GET <authority>/.well-known/openid-configuration ; compare redirect URIs; inspect token aud/iss.
items.knownFix: Register exact redirect URI, align issuer/audience, allow clock skew, ensure JWKS reachable.
items.preventionRule: Single source of truth for issuer/audience/redirect; trust JWKS by discovery.
items.detectBy: OIDC redirect/signature/issuer error.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: verify redirect URI, issuer/audience, and JWKS first.

items.title: Entra ID (Azure AD) claims-mapping failure
items.description: Symptoms: a user authenticates via Entra ID but the app sees missing roles/groups, an empty name/email, or authorization fails despite a valid token. Root cause: the expected claim (roles, groups, email) is not in the token because app roles/optional claims were not configured, group claims are emitted as object IDs not names, or the app reads the wrong claim type. Diagnostic: decode the issued token and inspect its claims; compare to the app's authorization policy claim names. Known fix: configure app roles/optional claims in the app registration, map group object IDs to roles in the app, and read the correct claim type. Prevention rule: define a claims contract and map it explicitly; never assume display names in tokens. LocalAIFactory detection: authenticated but authorization-empty. Fix/warn: warn to inspect the token claims and align the claims mapping before changing policy code.
items.applicability: Apps federating to Microsoft Entra ID for sign-in and authorization.
items.example: Roles empty because app roles were never assigned; configuring app roles populates the roles claim.
items.limitation: Large group memberships may be sent as a Graph reference (overage) rather than inline claims.
items.symptoms: Authenticated but missing roles/groups/email, or authorization fails with a valid token.
items.rootCause: App roles/optional claims not configured, group object IDs vs names, or wrong claim type read.
items.diagnosticCommand: Decode the issued token; inspect claims; compare to the app's policy claim names.
items.knownFix: Configure app roles/optional claims, map group IDs to roles, read the correct claim type.
items.preventionRule: Define and explicitly map a claims contract; never assume display names.
items.detectBy: Authenticated session with empty authorization claims.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: inspect token claims and align mapping before changing policy code.

items.title: Reverse-proxy / forwarded-headers misconfiguration
items.description: Symptoms: behind a reverse proxy (IIS ARR, Nginx) the app generates http:// redirect URLs instead of https://, sees the proxy IP as the client, or OIDC redirect URIs are wrong. Root cause: the app does not honor X-Forwarded-For/Proto, ForwardedHeaders middleware is not enabled (or not configured with known proxies/networks), so the scheme/host/client IP are taken from the proxy hop. Diagnostic: log the request scheme/host and remote IP as seen by the app and compare to what the proxy sends. Known fix: enable ForwardedHeaders middleware early in the pipeline and configure KnownProxies/KnownNetworks; ensure the proxy actually sets the forwarded headers. Prevention rule: configure forwarded headers whenever a proxy terminates TLS. LocalAIFactory detection: http redirects or wrong client IP behind a proxy. Fix/warn: warn to enable/configure forwarded headers before debugging redirect/auth logic.
items.applicability: Apps behind TLS-terminating reverse proxies or load balancers.
items.example: OIDC redirect built as http:// because the proxy terminated TLS; forwarded headers restore the https scheme.
items.limitation: Trusting forwarded headers from unknown proxies is a spoofing risk; restrict to known proxies.
items.symptoms: http redirects, proxy IP seen as client, or wrong OIDC redirect URI behind a proxy.
items.rootCause: Forwarded headers not honored/configured; scheme/host/IP taken from the proxy hop.
items.diagnosticCommand: Log request scheme/host and remote IP as the app sees them; compare to proxy headers.
items.knownFix: Enable ForwardedHeaders middleware early; configure KnownProxies/KnownNetworks; ensure the proxy sets headers.
items.preventionRule: Configure forwarded headers whenever a proxy terminates TLS.
items.detectBy: http redirects or wrong client IP behind a proxy.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: enable/configure forwarded headers before debugging redirect/auth logic.

items.title: CORS / auth-cookie cross-site failure
items.description: Symptoms: browser blocks a cross-origin request ('No Access-Control-Allow-Origin'), or an auth cookie is not sent cross-site so the user appears logged out on API calls. Root cause: CORS policy does not allow the origin/credentials, or the auth cookie lacks SameSite=None; Secure (required for cross-site cookies over HTTPS), or credentials: 'include' is not set on the client. Diagnostic: inspect the preflight response headers and the Set-Cookie attributes; confirm the request includes credentials. Known fix: configure a CORS policy with the specific origin and AllowCredentials, set the cookie SameSite=None; Secure over HTTPS, and send credentials from the client. Prevention rule: scope CORS to known origins (never * with credentials) and set cookie attributes deliberately. LocalAIFactory detection: blocked CORS or dropped auth cookie cross-site. Fix/warn: warn to align CORS origin/credentials and cookie SameSite/Secure first.
items.applicability: SPA/API setups where the browser frontend is a different origin from the API.
items.example: User logs out on API calls because the cookie was SameSite=Lax; setting SameSite=None; Secure fixes cross-site send.
items.limitation: SameSite=None requires Secure (HTTPS); will not work over plain HTTP.
items.symptoms: CORS block or auth cookie not sent cross-site.
items.rootCause: CORS policy/credentials misconfig, or cookie SameSite/Secure wrong, or no credentials:include.
items.diagnosticCommand: Inspect preflight headers and Set-Cookie attributes; confirm the request sends credentials.
items.knownFix: CORS policy with specific origin + AllowCredentials; cookie SameSite=None;Secure; client sends credentials.
items.preventionRule: Scope CORS to known origins (never * with credentials); set cookie attributes deliberately.
items.detectBy: Blocked CORS request or dropped auth cookie cross-site.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: align CORS origin/credentials and cookie SameSite/Secure first.

items.title: Upload-size / request-limit failure
items.description: Symptoms: large file uploads fail with 404.13 (request entity too large) under IIS, a 413, or a 400 with 'request body too large' from Kestrel. Root cause: IIS maxAllowedContentLength too small, Kestrel/ASP.NET Core MaxRequestBodySize or multipart limits too low, or a form value/length-limit cap. Diagnostic: reproduce with a file just over the suspected limit and read the exact status (404.13 vs Kestrel 413/400) to locate which layer rejects it. Known fix: raise maxAllowedContentLength in web.config and the corresponding Kestrel/MVC body and multipart limits, consistently across both layers. Prevention rule: set upload limits explicitly and test at boundary sizes; keep limits as low as the use case allows. LocalAIFactory detection: 404.13/413/400 on upload. Fix/warn: warn to align IIS and Kestrel request-size limits before code changes.
items.applicability: Apps accepting file uploads through IIS-fronted ASP.NET Core.
items.example: 404.13 because IIS maxAllowedContentLength was below the file size while Kestrel allowed it; aligning both fixes it.
items.limitation: Raising limits increases DoS exposure; pair with validation and streaming.
items.symptoms: 404.13, 413, or 400 'request body too large' on upload.
items.rootCause: IIS maxAllowedContentLength or Kestrel/MVC body/multipart limit too low (or mismatched).
items.diagnosticCommand: Upload a file just over the limit and read the exact status to identify the rejecting layer.
items.knownFix: Raise maxAllowedContentLength and Kestrel/MVC body+multipart limits consistently.
items.preventionRule: Set upload limits explicitly, test at boundaries, keep as low as feasible.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: align IIS and Kestrel request-size limits before code changes.

items.title: Database timeout / connection-pool exhaustion
items.description: Symptoms: intermittent 'timeout expired', 'the connection pool has been exhausted', or page hangs under load. Root cause: connections/DbContexts not disposed (leaked from the pool), long-running or unindexed queries holding connections, or — per this project's hard-won lesson — GroupBy(_ => 1) aggregate projections and large-text materialization causing unbounded client-side work that the command timeout does not catch. Diagnostic: check for undisposed scopes/contexts, inspect slow queries, and look for group-by-constant aggregates or list queries materializing large text columns. Known fix: dispose contexts (scoped lifetime), use separate CountAsync() calls instead of GroupBy(_ => 1), project list views to lightweight records, and add indexes. Prevention rule: never put blocking external calls or large-text loads on the request path; use lightweight read models. LocalAIFactory detection: a 'started' request log with no matching 'completed' line (a hang), or pool-exhaustion errors. Fix/warn: warn to remove group-by-constant and large-text loads and fix leaks before scaling the pool.
items.applicability: Any EF Core + SQL Server app, especially list/dashboard pages under load.
items.example: A page hung because a list query materialized a large Content column; projecting to a lightweight record fixed it.
items.limitation: Pool sizing tuning is secondary; leaks and bad queries are the usual root cause.
items.symptoms: Timeout expired, pool exhausted, or a page hang under load.
items.rootCause: Undisposed contexts, slow/unindexed queries, GroupBy(_ => 1) aggregates, or large-text materialization.
items.diagnosticCommand: Find undisposed scopes; inspect slow queries; search for GroupBy(_ => 1) and large-text list loads.
items.knownFix: Dispose contexts, use separate CountAsync(), project to lightweight records, add indexes.
items.preventionRule: No blocking external calls or large-text loads on the request path; use lightweight read models.
items.detectBy: A 'started' request log with no matching 'completed' line, or pool-exhaustion errors.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: remove group-by-constant and large-text loads and fix leaks before scaling the pool.

items.title: High-memory benchmark failure (OOM under load)
items.description: Symptoms: a benchmark or batch job crashes with OutOfMemoryException or the process is killed, especially when processing many documents/embeddings. Root cause: loading entire datasets/files/embeddings into memory at once, accumulating results in unbounded lists, or large object heap fragmentation. Diagnostic: capture peak working set and GC stats, identify the largest allocations, and check for unbounded accumulation. Known fix: stream and batch processing, use IAsyncEnumerable/paging, release references between batches, and cap concurrency. Prevention rule: bound memory by design (batch size, streaming) and measure peak memory in the benchmark itself. LocalAIFactory detection: OOM or working-set spike in a benchmark run. Fix/warn: warn to stream/batch and cap concurrency rather than adding RAM.
items.applicability: Embedding, ingestion, and analysis benchmarks over large corpora.
items.example: An embedding benchmark OOMs accumulating all vectors in a list; batching and flushing per chunk fixes it.
items.limitation: Very large single documents may still exceed memory; need chunk-level streaming.
items.symptoms: OutOfMemoryException or the process killed during a benchmark.
items.rootCause: Whole-dataset loads, unbounded result accumulation, or LOH fragmentation.
items.diagnosticCommand: Capture peak working set + GC stats; find the largest allocations.
items.knownFix: Stream/batch, use paging/IAsyncEnumerable, release per batch, cap concurrency.
items.preventionRule: Bound memory by design; measure peak memory in the benchmark.
items.detectBy: OOM or working-set spike in a benchmark run.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: stream/batch and cap concurrency rather than adding RAM.

items.title: Production incident rollback failure
items.description: Symptoms: a rollback after a bad release does not restore service — the previous build is gone, a forward-only migration cannot be reversed, or config/secrets from the new version persist. Root cause: no retained previous artifact, a destructive (non-reversible) migration, or rollback that reverts code but not configuration/data. Diagnostic: confirm the previous deployable artifact and the database state are both restorable, and whether the failed release applied a one-way migration. Known fix: redeploy the retained previous artifact, restore from a pre-release backup if a destructive migration ran, and revert config together with code. Prevention rule: keep the previous artifact, take a pre-release backup, and prefer additive/reversible migrations so rollback is clean. LocalAIFactory detection: rollback step fails or service stays down after revert. Fix/warn: warn to restore the retained artifact + pre-release backup and revert config, and to flag any destructive migration that blocks rollback.
items.applicability: Release/rollback runbooks for the deployed app and its database.
items.example: Rollback failed because a destructive migration dropped a column; restoring the pre-release backup recovered service.
items.limitation: Data written by the new version after a destructive change may be unrecoverable.
items.symptoms: Rollback does not restore service; missing prior artifact or irreversible migration.
items.rootCause: No retained artifact, destructive migration, or config/data not reverted with code.
items.diagnosticCommand: Confirm the previous artifact + DB backup are restorable; check if the release applied a one-way migration.
items.knownFix: Redeploy retained artifact, restore pre-release backup for destructive changes, revert config with code.
items.preventionRule: Keep the previous artifact, take a pre-release backup, prefer additive/reversible migrations.
items.detectBy: Rollback step fails or service stays down after revert.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: restore artifact + backup, revert config, and flag destructive migrations blocking rollback.

items.title: Invoke-WebRequest does not send credentials over plain HTTP
items.description: Symptoms: a scripted health/login check to a Windows-auth endpoint over http:// returns 401 even with -UseDefaultCredentials or -Credential, while a browser succeeds. Firsthand finding in this project: PowerShell's Invoke-WebRequest will not silently send Windows credentials over an unencrypted HTTP connection by default, so an integrated-auth probe over http fails where the same request over https or via a browser works. Root cause: the client refuses to transmit credentials over an unprotected channel. Diagnostic: compare the same request over https vs http and with/without credentials. Known fix: probe over HTTPS, or use a client/configuration that permits credentialed HTTP only in a trusted local context, or test auth via a browser/curl with explicit settings. Prevention rule: run authenticated probes over HTTPS. LocalAIFactory detection: scripted 401 on a Windows-auth endpoint that works in a browser. Fix/warn: warn that the probe transport (HTTP) is the cause, not the app's auth.
items.applicability: Deployment/health scripts probing Windows-authenticated endpoints with PowerShell.
items.example: A health check 401'd over http while the browser succeeded; switching the probe to https authenticated correctly.
items.limitation: Behavior depends on the PowerShell/.NET version and client policy.
items.symptoms: Scripted 401 over http to a Windows-auth endpoint that works in a browser.
items.rootCause: The HTTP client refuses to send credentials over an unencrypted channel.
items.diagnosticCommand: Run the same request over https vs http, with and without credentials, and compare.
items.knownFix: Probe over HTTPS, or test auth via a browser/curl with explicit settings.
items.preventionRule: Run authenticated probes over HTTPS.
items.detectBy: Scripted 401 on a Windows-auth endpoint that succeeds in a browser.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: the probe transport (HTTP) is the cause, not the app's auth.

items.title: appcmd attribute path quirk (config path quoting/escaping)
items.description: Symptoms: appcmd set config commands silently no-op, target the wrong section, or fail to parse when a path/attribute contains spaces, slashes, or a colon. Firsthand finding in this project: appcmd's attribute/section path syntax is finicky — the exact /section, /commit:apphost, and attribute path quoting must be precise or the change appears to succeed but does not apply. Root cause: incorrect section path, missing /commit scope, or unquoted paths with special characters in the attribute value. Diagnostic: appcmd list config to read the current effective value and confirm the change actually applied. Known fix: quote attribute values, specify the correct /section and /commit:apphost, and verify with a follow-up list config. Prevention rule: always read back the config after an appcmd set to confirm it applied. LocalAIFactory detection: an appcmd 'success' with no effective change. Fix/warn: warn to quote paths, set the right section/commit, and verify with list config.
items.applicability: Scripted IIS configuration changes via appcmd.
items.example: An appcmd set reported success but the binding was unchanged because the section/commit scope was wrong; correcting it applied the change.
items.limitation: appcmd behavior varies by IIS version; verify on the target host.
