items.symptoms: appcmd set no-ops, wrong section, or parse failure with special-character paths.
items.rootCause: Wrong section path, missing /commit scope, or unquoted special-character values.
items.diagnosticCommand: appcmd list config to read the effective value and confirm the change applied.
items.knownFix: Quote values, use correct /section and /commit:apphost, verify with list config.
items.preventionRule: Always read back config after appcmd set.
items.detectBy: appcmd 'success' with no effective change.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: quote paths, set the right section/commit, verify with list config.

items.title: Entra ID / OIDC token validation and missing claims
items.description: Symptoms: 401 after a seemingly successful sign-in, IDX10214/IDX10205 issuer or audience validation errors, or an authenticated user missing expected role/group claims. Root cause: the app validates the wrong issuer or audience, the signing keys are stale (key rollover), the host clock is skewed, or the requested scopes/claims were not configured so the token never carried them. Diagnostic: decode the id_token/access_token and compare iss, aud, exp against the discovery document at .well-known/openid-configuration. Known fix: align ValidIssuer/ValidAudience with the tenant and app registration, let the middleware refresh JWKS, sync the clock, and request the scopes that emit the needed claims. Prevention rule: drive issuer/keys from discovery rather than hardcoding, and assert required claims at startup. LocalAIFactory detection: post-login 401 with IDX token-validation codes. Fix/warn: warn that token validation/claims config is the cause, not the app's authorization policy.
items.applicability: ASP.NET Core apps using Entra ID / OpenID Connect for sign-in, especially after tenant or app-registration changes.
items.example: A user signs in but is denied because aud in the token does not match the configured audience; aligning ValidAudience resolves it.
items.limitation: Does not cover B2C custom policies or token-exchange flows, which add their own validation rules.
items.symptoms: Post-login 401, IDX issuer/audience errors, or expected claims absent.
items.rootCause: Wrong issuer/audience, stale JWKS, clock skew, or unconfigured scopes/claims.
items.diagnosticCommand: curl -s https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant>/v2.0/.well-known/openid-configuration | jq '.issuer, .jwks_uri' ; then decode the JWT and compare iss/aud/exp.
items.knownFix: Set ValidIssuer/ValidAudience from discovery, allow JWKS refresh, sync clock, request needed scopes.
items.preventionRule: Drive issuer and signing keys from discovery; assert required claims at startup.
items.detectBy: Post-login 401 with IDX10205/IDX10214 or missing-claim authorization failures.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: token validation/claims configuration, not an app authorization bug.

items.title: CORS and SameSite cookie failures for cross-site auth
items.description: Symptoms: browser console shows 'blocked by CORS policy', preflight OPTIONS returns 403/no Access-Control-Allow-Origin, or the auth/session cookie is silently dropped on cross-site requests. Root cause: the server omits the matching CORS origin/credentials headers, or the cookie lacks SameSite=None;Secure so the browser refuses to send it cross-site. Diagnostic: inspect the OPTIONS preflight and the Set-Cookie attributes in the network tab; reproduce with curl -X OPTIONS including Origin and Access-Control-Request-Method. Known fix: configure an explicit allowed-origins policy with AllowCredentials when cookies are used, and set SameSite=None;Secure (over HTTPS) for cross-site cookies. Prevention rule: never use wildcard origin with credentials; pin exact origins and require HTTPS for None cookies. LocalAIFactory detection: preflight failures or dropped cookies on cross-origin calls. Fix/warn: warn this is CORS/cookie policy, not backend logic.
items.applicability: SPAs or partner sites calling an API on a different origin, or auth flows that set cookies across sites.
items.example: A cookie set without SameSite=None is not sent on a cross-site fetch, so the user appears logged out; adding None;Secure fixes it.
items.limitation: Browser SameSite defaults and partitioned-cookie behavior evolve; verify against current browser versions.
items.symptoms: CORS-blocked requests, failed preflight, or cross-site cookie not sent.
items.rootCause: Missing CORS origin/credentials headers or cookie lacking SameSite=None;Secure.
items.diagnosticCommand: curl -i -X OPTIONS https://api.example.local/resource -H 'Origin: https://app.example.local' -H 'Access-Control-Request-Method: GET'
items.knownFix: Configure explicit allowed origins with AllowCredentials; set SameSite=None;Secure over HTTPS.
items.preventionRule: No wildcard origin with credentials; pin exact origins; require HTTPS for SameSite=None.
items.detectBy: Preflight without Access-Control-Allow-Origin or Set-Cookie dropped cross-site.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: CORS/cookie policy issue, not backend logic.

items.title: Forwarded headers behind a reverse proxy (wrong scheme/IP)
items.description: Symptoms: redirect URIs come out as http instead of https, the OIDC redirect mismatches, client IPs all show as the proxy address, or secure cookies are not set behind a load balancer or reverse proxy. Root cause: the app does not honor X-Forwarded-Proto/X-Forwarded-For, so it sees the proxy's internal HTTP hop rather than the original HTTPS request. Diagnostic: log Request.Scheme and RemoteIpAddress and compare against the proxy's forwarded headers. Known fix: enable ForwardedHeaders middleware early in the pipeline, configure it to read XForwardedProto and XForwardedFor, and set KnownProxies/KnownNetworks to trusted hops. Prevention rule: configure forwarded-headers handling before authentication and trust only known proxies. LocalAIFactory detection: scheme-mismatch redirects or all-same client IP behind a proxy. Fix/warn: warn the proxy-header handling is the cause, not the app URL generation.
items.applicability: ASP.NET Core apps behind IIS ARR, nginx, or a load balancer that terminates TLS.
items.example: OIDC sign-in fails because the generated redirect_uri is http://; enabling forwarded headers restores https:// and the redirect matches.
items.limitation: Trusting forwarded headers from untrusted sources is a spoofing risk; only enable for known proxies.
items.symptoms: http instead of https redirects, proxy IP as client IP, or secure cookies not set.
items.rootCause: ForwardedHeaders not honored, so the app sees the internal HTTP hop.
items.diagnosticCommand: Log HttpContext.Request.Scheme and Connection.RemoteIpAddress; compare with X-Forwarded-Proto / X-Forwarded-For at the proxy.
items.knownFix: Enable ForwardedHeaders middleware early, read XForwardedProto/For, set KnownProxies/KnownNetworks.
items.preventionRule: Configure forwarded-headers before authentication; trust only known proxies.
items.detectBy: Scheme-mismatch redirects or uniform client IP behind a reverse proxy.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: proxy-header handling, not app URL generation.

items.title: IIS request/upload size limits (413 on large uploads)
items.description: Symptoms: large file uploads or big request bodies fail with HTTP 413 (Request Entity Too Large) or 404.13, while small requests succeed. Root cause: IIS caps request size via requestFiltering maxAllowedContentLength (default ~30 MB) and ASP.NET Core caps it via maxRequestBodySize; either limit can reject the body before the handler runs. Diagnostic: reproduce with a body just over the threshold and check whether the failure is 413 (ASP.NET Core) or 404.13 (IIS requestFiltering). Known fix: raise maxAllowedContentLength in web.config requestFiltering and set the Kestrel/IIS MaxRequestBodySize (or [RequestSizeLimit]) to the intended ceiling, keeping both consistent. Prevention rule: set an explicit, documented upload ceiling in both layers and validate it. LocalAIFactory detection: 413/404.13 correlated with payload size. Fix/warn: warn this is a size-limit config issue in IIS and the app, not a parsing bug.
items.applicability: Apps accepting file uploads or large payloads behind IIS, especially after migrating from Kestrel-only testing.
items.example: A 50 MB import fails with 404.13 until maxAllowedContentLength is raised and the app's request-body limit is increased to match.
items.limitation: Raising limits increases denial-of-service surface; pair with authentication, streaming, and antivirus on uploads.
items.symptoms: 413 or 404.13 on large uploads while small requests work.
items.rootCause: IIS maxAllowedContentLength and/or ASP.NET Core MaxRequestBodySize too low.
items.diagnosticCommand: Send a body just over the limit and observe 413 (app) vs 404.13 (IIS requestFiltering) in the response.
items.knownFix: Raise web.config requestFiltering maxAllowedContentLength and the app MaxRequestBodySize/[RequestSizeLimit] consistently.
items.preventionRule: Set an explicit upload ceiling in both IIS and the app; validate it.
items.detectBy: 413/404.13 correlated with payload size.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: size-limit configuration in IIS and app, not a parser bug.

items.title: Odoo external API auth and pagination/rate-limit failures
items.description: Symptoms: Odoo XML-RPC/JSON-RPC calls return AccessDenied, uid=false, partial result sets, or time out on large reads. Root cause: execute_kw is called before a successful common.authenticate so uid is false, the wrong database name is passed, or large search_read calls are issued without limit/offset so they overrun memory or a gateway timeout. Diagnostic: call common.version then common.authenticate to confirm uid, then page reads with limit/offset. Known fix: authenticate first, pin the exact database, store the api_key as a vault reference, and paginate large reads with limit and offset. Prevention rule: always obtain uid before object calls and never issue unbounded search_read. LocalAIFactory detection: AccessDenied or truncated/timed-out Odoo reads. Fix/warn: warn this is an auth-order or pagination issue, not a model error.
items.applicability: Integrations reading or writing Odoo data over its external API.
items.example: A bulk partner export times out until it is paged with limit=200 and incremented offset.
items.limitation: Odoo behavior varies by version and by Enterprise vs Community; verify on the target instance.
items.symptoms: AccessDenied, uid=false, truncated results, or timeouts on large Odoo reads.
items.rootCause: Call before authenticate, wrong database, or unbounded search_read.
items.diagnosticCommand: curl -s https://odoo.example.local/jsonrpc -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"call","params":{"service":"common","method":"authenticate","args":["db","user","<key-ref>",{}]}}'
items.knownFix: Authenticate first, pin the database, vault the api_key, paginate with limit/offset.
items.preventionRule: Obtain uid before object calls; never issue unbounded search_read.
items.detectBy: AccessDenied or truncated/timed-out Odoo responses.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: auth-order or pagination issue, not a model error.

items.title: WordPress REST API authentication and rate-limit failures
items.description: Symptoms: WordPress REST calls return 401 'rest_cannot_*', 403, or are throttled by a security plugin/host. Root cause: the Authorization header is stripped by the server (common with Apache/PHP-FPM), an Application Password is not used over HTTPS, or a WAF/security plugin rate-limits or blocks /wp-json. Diagnostic: GET /wp-json to confirm the API is reachable, then call an authenticated endpoint with an Application Password over HTTPS. Known fix: ensure the server forwards the Authorization header (SetEnvIf/CGIPassAuth), use Application Passwords over TLS, and allowlist the integration in the security plugin. Prevention rule: use Application Passwords or OAuth over HTTPS and confirm header forwarding. LocalAIFactory detection: 401 rest_cannot or sudden 403/429 from /wp-json. Fix/warn: warn this is auth-header forwarding or WAF throttling, not a content bug.
items.applicability: Integrations reading or posting to WordPress via its REST API.
items.example: Authenticated calls 401 because Apache dropped the Authorization header; enabling CGIPassAuth restores them.
items.limitation: Plugin and host security layers differ widely; verify the specific stack.
items.symptoms: 401 rest_cannot_*, 403, or throttling on /wp-json.
items.rootCause: Authorization header stripped, no Application Password over HTTPS, or WAF/plugin throttling.
items.diagnosticCommand: curl -s https://wp.example.local/wp-json/ | jq '.namespaces' ; then curl -u user:app-password https://wp.example.local/wp-json/wp/v2/posts
items.knownFix: Forward the Authorization header, use Application Passwords over TLS, allowlist the integration.
items.preventionRule: Application Passwords/OAuth over HTTPS; confirm header forwarding.
items.detectBy: 401 rest_cannot or 403/429 from /wp-json.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: auth-header forwarding or WAF throttling, not a content bug.

items.title: WooCommerce REST API key scope and pagination failures
items.description: Symptoms: WooCommerce REST calls return 401 'woocommerce_rest_authentication_error', 403 on writes, or only the first page of orders/products. Root cause: consumer key/secret are sent over HTTP instead of HTTPS (Woo requires query-string keys only over HTTPS or OAuth1.0a over HTTP), the key has read-only scope for a write, or the client ignores the X-WP-TotalPages header and stops at page 1. Diagnostic: call /wp-json/wc/v3/system_status with the key over HTTPS, then read X-WP-Total/X-WP-TotalPages. Known fix: use HTTPS with key/secret (or OAuth1.0a over HTTP), grant the correct read/write scope, and page using per_page plus the total-pages header. Prevention rule: provision keys with least-privilege scope and always paginate. LocalAIFactory detection: 401/403 or single-page result sets. Fix/warn: warn this is key scope/transport or pagination, not a data error.
items.applicability: Integrations syncing WooCommerce orders, products, or customers via the WC REST API.
items.example: A product sync writes 403 because the key is read-only; reissuing it with read/write scope fixes the writes.
items.limitation: WooCommerce and WordPress versions change auth specifics; verify on the target store.
items.symptoms: 401 woocommerce_rest_authentication_error, 403 on writes, or single-page results.
items.rootCause: Keys over HTTP, wrong key scope, or pagination ignored.
items.diagnosticCommand: curl -s -u ck_xxx:cs_xxx https://shop.example.local/wp-json/wc/v3/system_status | jq '.environment.version'
items.knownFix: Use HTTPS with keys (or OAuth1.0a over HTTP), correct read/write scope, paginate with per_page + total-pages header.
items.preventionRule: Least-privilege key scope; always paginate.
items.detectBy: 401/403 or single-page WooCommerce result sets.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: key scope/transport or pagination, not a data error.

items.title: ERPNext / Frappe API auth and rate-limit failures
items.description: Symptoms: ERPNext/Frappe API calls return 401/403 PermissionError, 417 on validation, or 429 when polling. Root cause: the Authorization 'token api_key:api_secret' header is malformed, the user/role lacks DocType permission, or rapid polling trips Frappe's rate limiting. Diagnostic: GET /api/method/frappe.auth.get_logged_user with the token header to confirm identity, then read a resource the role permits. Known fix: send 'Authorization: token <key>:<secret>', grant the role read/write on the DocType, and back off on 429 with retry-after. Prevention rule: use a dedicated API user with least-privilege roles and respect rate limits. LocalAIFactory detection: 401/403 PermissionError or 429 from /api. Fix/warn: warn this is token format, role permission, or rate limiting, not a data bug.
items.applicability: Integrations reading or writing ERPNext/Frappe documents via the REST/RPC API.
items.example: Calls 403 because the API user's role lacks read on Sales Invoice; granting the permission resolves it.
items.limitation: Frappe permission and rate-limit behavior varies by version and site config; verify on the instance.
items.symptoms: 401/403 PermissionError, 417 validation, or 429 rate limiting.
items.rootCause: Malformed token header, missing DocType permission, or rate-limit breach.
items.diagnosticCommand: curl -s -H 'Authorization: token <key>:<secret>' https://erp.example.local/api/method/frappe.auth.get_logged_user
items.knownFix: Use 'token key:secret' header, grant DocType permissions, back off on 429.
items.preventionRule: Dedicated least-privilege API user; respect rate limits.
items.detectBy: 401/403 PermissionError or 429 from /api.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: token format, role permission, or rate limiting, not a data bug.

items.title: Magento REST API token and pagination failures
items.description: Symptoms: Magento REST calls return 401 'consumer is not authorized', 4xx on searchCriteria, or only the first page of a catalog. Root cause: an expired/short-lived admin/integration token, missing ACL resources on the integration, or searchCriteria pageSize/currentPage not supplied so large result sets are truncated. Diagnostic: POST /rest/V1/integration/admin/token to mint a token, then GET a resource with searchCriteria paging. Known fix: refresh the token, grant the integration the required ACL resources, and page with searchCriteria[pageSize] and currentPage. Prevention rule: use an integration with explicit ACLs and always paginate searchCriteria queries. LocalAIFactory detection: 401 consumer-not-authorized or truncated catalogs. Fix/warn: warn this is token/ACL or pagination, not a catalog error.
items.applicability: Integrations syncing Magento (Adobe Commerce) products, orders, or customers via REST.
items.example: A product pull returns 100 of 5000 items because pageSize/currentPage were omitted; adding searchCriteria paging retrieves all.
items.limitation: Magento Open Source vs Adobe Commerce and version differences affect endpoints; verify on the target.
items.symptoms: 401 consumer-not-authorized, ACL 4xx, or single-page catalogs.
items.rootCause: Expired token, missing ACL resources, or searchCriteria paging omitted.
items.diagnosticCommand: curl -s -X POST https://magento.example.local/rest/V1/integration/admin/token -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"username":"u","password":"<ref>"}'
items.knownFix: Refresh token, grant ACL resources, page with searchCriteria pageSize/currentPage.
items.preventionRule: Integration with explicit ACLs; always paginate searchCriteria.
items.detectBy: 401 consumer-not-authorized or truncated Magento result sets.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: token/ACL or pagination, not a catalog error.

items.title: Drupal JSON:API auth and pagination failures
items.description: Symptoms: Drupal JSON:API calls return 403 (access denied), 406 Not Acceptable, or paged collections that the client fails to follow. Root cause: the JSON:API module is read-only by default or the role lacks permission, the Accept/Content-Type header is not application/vnd.api+json, or the client ignores the links.next cursor for pagination. Diagnostic: GET /jsonapi with the vnd.api+json Accept header, then follow links.next on a collection. Known fix: grant the role the needed permissions (and enable write if required), send the correct media type, and follow links.next until absent. Prevention rule: send vnd.api+json, use least-privilege roles, and paginate via links.next. LocalAIFactory detection: 403/406 or truncated JSON:API collections. Fix/warn: warn this is permissions/media-type or pagination, not a content bug.
items.applicability: Integrations reading or writing Drupal content via JSON:API.
items.example: A request 406s because Accept was application/json; switching to application/vnd.api+json succeeds.
items.limitation: Drupal permission models and contrib modules vary; verify on the site.
items.symptoms: 403 access denied, 406 Not Acceptable, or unfollowed pagination.
items.rootCause: Missing permission/read-only module, wrong media type, or links.next ignored.
items.diagnosticCommand: curl -s -H 'Accept: application/vnd.api+json' https://drupal.example.local/jsonapi
items.knownFix: Grant role permissions, send vnd.api+json, follow links.next.
items.preventionRule: Correct media type; least-privilege roles; paginate via links.next.
items.detectBy: 403/406 or truncated JSON:API collections.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: permissions/media-type or pagination, not a content bug.

items.title: Keycloak admin REST token and realm/client errors
items.description: Symptoms: Keycloak admin REST calls return 401, 403 'unknown_error'/insufficient scope, or 404 on a realm/client. Root cause: the access token came from the wrong realm or client, the service account lacks the realm-management roles (e.g. manage-users), or the wrong base path is used (newer Keycloak drops the /auth prefix). Diagnostic: obtain a token from the correct realm's token endpoint, decode it to confirm audience/roles, then call the admin endpoint. Known fix: use the right realm and client, grant the service account the required realm-management roles, and use the correct base path for the Keycloak version. Prevention rule: pin realm/client and grant least-privilege realm-management roles. LocalAIFactory detection: 401/403 from /admin/realms. Fix/warn: warn this is token realm/role or base-path, not a data bug.
items.applicability: Integrations administering Keycloak realms, clients, or users via the admin REST API.
items.example: A user-list call 403s because the service account lacks view-users; granting it fixes the call.
items.limitation: The /auth base-path and role names changed across Keycloak versions; verify on the target.
items.symptoms: 401, 403 insufficient scope, or 404 on realm/client paths.
items.rootCause: Wrong realm/client token, missing realm-management roles, or wrong base path.
items.diagnosticCommand: curl -s -X POST https://kc.example.local/realms/<realm>/protocol/openid-connect/token -d 'grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=<id>&client_secret=<ref>'
items.knownFix: Use correct realm/client, grant realm-management roles, correct base path for the version.
items.preventionRule: Pin realm/client; least-privilege realm-management roles.
items.detectBy: 401/403 from /admin/realms endpoints.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: token realm/role or base-path, not a data bug.

items.title: Grafana data source and dashboard provisioning failures
items.description: Symptoms: Grafana panels show 'No data', a data source test fails, or provisioned dashboards do not appear. Root cause: the data source URL is unreachable from the Grafana container/host (localhost vs service name), the auth/token is wrong, or the provisioning YAML path is not mounted/loaded. Diagnostic: use the Grafana data source 'test' endpoint or /api/health, and confirm provisioning files are present in the configured path. Known fix: set the data source URL to a name reachable from Grafana, supply correct credentials, and mount provisioning config at the expected path. Prevention rule: provision data sources and dashboards as code and test connectivity from Grafana's network context. LocalAIFactory detection: 'No data' panels or failed data source tests. Fix/warn: warn this is connectivity/provisioning, not a query bug.
items.applicability: Observability stacks using Grafana with provisioned data sources and dashboards.
items.example: Panels show no data because the Prometheus URL was localhost inside the Grafana container; using the service name fixes it.
items.limitation: Networking specifics depend on deployment (container, host, VM); verify the actual topology.
items.symptoms: No data panels, failed data source test, or missing provisioned dashboards.
items.rootCause: Unreachable data source URL, wrong credentials, or unmounted provisioning path.
items.diagnosticCommand: curl -s http://grafana.example.local/api/health ; verify data source URL is reachable from Grafana's network context.
items.knownFix: Set a reachable data source URL, correct credentials, mount provisioning config at the expected path.
items.preventionRule: Provision as code; test connectivity from Grafana's network context.
items.detectBy: No-data panels or failed data source tests.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: connectivity/provisioning, not a query bug.

items.title: Prometheus scrape target down or relabel misconfiguration
items.description: Symptoms: a target shows DOWN on Prometheus /targets, metrics are missing, or labels are unexpected. Root cause: the scrape target URL/port is wrong or firewalled, the /metrics path differs, the scrape interval/timeout is too short, or a relabel_config drops or rewrites the target. Diagnostic: open /targets to read the lastError per target and curl the target's /metrics directly. Known fix: correct the target address/path, open the port, tune scrape_interval/timeout, and review relabel_configs. Prevention rule: validate scrape config and reachability before relying on the metrics. LocalAIFactory detection: DOWN targets or gaps in expected series. Fix/warn: warn this is scrape config/reachability, not a metric-emission bug.
items.applicability: Observability stacks scraping application or exporter metrics with Prometheus.
items.example: A target is DOWN because the exporter listens on a different port than configured; correcting the port restores scraping.
items.limitation: Service-discovery setups add their own relabel complexity; verify the discovery source.
items.symptoms: Target DOWN, missing metrics, or unexpected labels.
items.rootCause: Wrong target/port/path, firewall, short timeout, or relabel dropping the target.
items.diagnosticCommand: curl -s http://app.example.local:9100/metrics | head ; check Prometheus /targets lastError.
items.knownFix: Correct target address/path, open the port, tune scrape timeout, review relabel_configs.
items.preventionRule: Validate scrape config and reachability before relying on metrics.
items.detectBy: DOWN targets or gaps in expected series.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: scrape config/reachability, not a metric-emission bug.

items.title: OpenTelemetry exporter endpoint and protocol mismatch
items.description: Symptoms: no traces/metrics reach the collector, exporter logs show connection refused or 'failed to export', or spans silently vanish. Root cause: the OTLP endpoint/port is wrong (4317 gRPC vs 4318 HTTP), the protocol (grpc vs http/protobuf) does not match the collector, TLS is expected but plaintext is sent, or the collector pipeline lacks a matching receiver/exporter. Diagnostic: confirm the collector is listening on the configured OTLP port and that the exporter protocol matches; check collector logs for received spans. Known fix: align OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT and protocol with the collector, fix TLS settings, and ensure the collector pipeline routes the signal. Prevention rule: pin endpoint, port, and protocol consistently across app and collector. LocalAIFactory detection: telemetry absent despite instrumentation. Fix/warn: warn this is exporter endpoint/protocol config, not an instrumentation bug.
items.applicability: Apps exporting OpenTelemetry traces/metrics/logs to a collector or backend.
items.example: Spans never arrive because the app sends gRPC to the HTTP port 4318; switching to 4317 (or to http/protobuf) fixes it.
items.limitation: Collector pipeline and backend specifics vary; verify the receiver/exporter wiring.
items.symptoms: No telemetry received, connection refused, or silently dropped spans.
items.rootCause: Wrong OTLP port/protocol, TLS mismatch, or collector pipeline gap.
items.diagnosticCommand: Confirm the collector listens on the OTLP port (e.g. 4317/4318); check collector logs for 'received' spans against OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT.
items.knownFix: Align endpoint, port, and protocol; fix TLS; ensure the collector pipeline routes the signal.
items.preventionRule: Pin endpoint, port, and protocol consistently across app and collector.
items.detectBy: Telemetry absent despite active instrumentation.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: exporter endpoint/protocol config, not an instrumentation bug.

items.title: SMTP TLS/auth failure (STARTTLS not offered or 535)
items.description: Symptoms: mail fails with 535 authentication failure, the client refuses to send because STARTTLS is not offered, or messages are rejected with 550 relaying denied. Root cause: wrong credentials, the submission server does not advertise STARTTLS so credentials would go in clear (and the client correctly aborts), the wrong port is used (25 vs 587 vs 465), or the sender is not permitted to relay. Diagnostic: openssl s_client -starttls smtp -connect host:587 to confirm TLS and capabilities, then test AUTH. Known fix: use port 587 with STARTTLS (or 465 implicit TLS), correct credentials, and a permitted MAIL FROM. Prevention rule: require TLS before AUTH, vault the password, and use an SPF/DKIM-aligned sender. LocalAIFactory detection: 535/550 or STARTTLS-absent aborts. Fix/warn: warn this is SMTP TLS/auth/relay config, not an app message bug.
items.applicability: Apps sending notification or report email via an SMTP submission server.
items.example: Sending aborts because the server on port 25 does not offer STARTTLS; switching to 587 with STARTTLS resolves it.
items.limitation: Mail-server policies and anti-spam layers differ; verify with the mail administrator.
items.symptoms: 535 auth failure, STARTTLS-absent abort, or 550 relaying denied.
items.rootCause: Wrong credentials, no STARTTLS, wrong port, or sender not permitted to relay.
items.diagnosticCommand: openssl s_client -starttls smtp -connect smtp.example.local:587 -crlf
items.knownFix: Use 587 STARTTLS or 465 implicit TLS, correct credentials, permitted MAIL FROM.
items.preventionRule: Require TLS before AUTH; vault the password; use SPF/DKIM-aligned sender.
items.detectBy: 535/550 responses or STARTTLS-absent aborts.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: SMTP TLS/auth/relay config, not an app message bug.

items.title: SFTP host-key verification and connection failures
items.description: Symptoms: SFTP transfers fail with 'host key verification failed', 'no matching key exchange/host-key algorithm', or authentication is rejected. Root cause: the server's host key is not in known_hosts or has legitimately changed, the client and server share no common key-exchange/host-key algorithm (older server vs newer client), or key/password auth is misconfigured. Diagnostic: ssh -v or sftp with verbose logging to see the offered algorithms and host-key fingerprint. Known fix: add/refresh the verified host key, enable a mutually supported algorithm where safe, and confirm the auth method (key vs password). Prevention rule: pin and verify host-key fingerprints out of band; never disable host-key checking. LocalAIFactory detection: host-key or algorithm-mismatch errors at connect. Fix/warn: warn this is host-key/algorithm/auth config, not a transfer-logic bug.
items.applicability: Batch file exchange over SFTP with banking or partner endpoints.
items.example: A transfer fails because the server uses an older host-key algorithm the client disabled; enabling it (or updating the server) restores the connection.
items.limitation: Disabling host-key checking to 'fix' this is a security risk and must not be done; verify fingerprints instead.
items.symptoms: Host key verification failed, no matching algorithm, or auth rejected.
items.rootCause: Unknown/changed host key, no common algorithm, or misconfigured auth method.
items.diagnosticCommand: ssh -v sftp-user@sftp.example.local (or sftp with -v) to inspect offered algorithms and the host-key fingerprint.
items.knownFix: Add/refresh the verified host key, enable a mutually supported algorithm, confirm auth method.
items.preventionRule: Pin and verify host-key fingerprints out of band; never disable host-key checking.
items.detectBy: Host-key or algorithm-mismatch errors at connect.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: host-key/algorithm/auth config, not a transfer-logic bug.

items.title: EF Core migration concurrency and lock during deploy
items.description: Symptoms: startup migration hangs or fails when two app instances start together, with a lock-timeout or 'a migration is already in progress'. Root cause: multiple instances race to apply migrations against the __EFMigrationsHistory table, or a long-running migration holds a schema lock that blocks other connections. Diagnostic: query sys.dm_tran_locks and the migrations-history table; check application logs for which instance acquired the migration. Known fix: serialize migrations so only one instance (or a deploy step) applies them, apply during a controlled window, and keep migrations small and additive. Prevention rule: run migrations as a single ordered deploy step, not concurrently from every instance. LocalAIFactory detection: startup migration lock-timeout with multiple instances. Fix/warn: warn to serialize migration application, not to retry blindly.
items.applicability: Multi-instance ASP.NET Core deployments that migrate on startup against shared SQL Server.
items.example: Two instances start simultaneously; one blocks on a schema lock until migrations are serialized to a single deploy step.
items.limitation: Single-instance deployments rarely hit this; the risk grows with scale-out.
items.symptoms: Migration hang or lock-timeout when instances start together.
items.rootCause: Concurrent migration race or a long migration holding a schema lock.
items.diagnosticCommand: SELECT request_session_id, resource_type, request_mode FROM sys.dm_tran_locks WHERE request_status = 'WAIT';
items.knownFix: Serialize migrations to one instance/deploy step, apply in a controlled window, keep migrations additive.
items.preventionRule: Run migrations as a single ordered deploy step, not from every instance.
items.detectBy: Startup migration lock-timeout with multiple instances.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: serialize migration application, do not retry blindly.

items.title: SQL Server connection-pool exhaustion
items.description: Symptoms: intermittent 'Timeout expired. The timeout period elapsed prior to obtaining a connection from the pool', rising latency under load, then recovery when traffic drops. Root cause: connections are not disposed (missing using/await using), a long-running query holds connections, or the pool Max Pool Size is too small for concurrency. Diagnostic: query sys.dm_exec_sessions/connections to count active sessions per login and watch for growth; review code for undisposed DbContext/SqlConnection. Known fix: dispose connections deterministically, shorten or async-ify long queries, and size Max Pool Size to real concurrency. Prevention rule: always dispose DbContext/connections and keep request-path queries short. LocalAIFactory detection: pool-timeout errors that correlate with load. Fix/warn: warn this is connection lifetime/pool sizing, not a query-result bug.
items.applicability: Apps under concurrent load using ADO.NET/EF Core connection pooling against SQL Server.
items.example: Pool timeouts appear under load because a background loop never disposed its DbContext; fixing disposal clears the timeouts.
items.limitation: Raising Max Pool Size without fixing leaks only delays exhaustion; address the leak first.
items.symptoms: Connection-pool timeout errors and rising latency under load.
items.rootCause: Undisposed connections, long-running queries, or undersized pool.
items.diagnosticCommand: SELECT login_name, COUNT(*) FROM sys.dm_exec_sessions GROUP BY login_name; watch active-session growth under load.
items.knownFix: Dispose connections deterministically, shorten/async-ify long queries, right-size Max Pool Size.
items.preventionRule: Always dispose DbContext/connections; keep request-path queries short.
items.detectBy: Pool-timeout errors correlated with load.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: connection lifetime/pool sizing, not a query-result bug.

items.title: TLS cipher/protocol mismatch (handshake failure)
items.description: Symptoms: HTTPS or service-to-service calls fail with 'could not create SSL/TLS secure channel', handshake_failure, or 'protocol version' errors. Root cause: the client and server share no common TLS protocol version or cipher suite — typically an older endpoint requiring TLS 1.0/1.1 while the platform disabled them, or a cipher policy mismatch. Diagnostic: openssl s_client -connect host:443 -tls1_2 (and -tls1_3) to see which versions/ciphers negotiate. Known fix: enable a mutually supported, still-secure protocol/cipher where unavoidable, or upgrade the older endpoint; align the OS/.NET TLS policy with the endpoint's minimum. Prevention rule: standardize on TLS 1.2+ and inventory endpoints that cannot meet it. LocalAIFactory detection: SSL/TLS secure-channel or handshake errors. Fix/warn: warn this is TLS protocol/cipher negotiation, not an app certificate-trust bug.
items.applicability: Outbound HTTPS/service calls to endpoints with differing TLS policy from the host.
items.example: A call fails handshake because the partner only supports TLS 1.1 which the host disabled; upgrading the partner (or a scoped exception) resolves it.
items.limitation: Re-enabling deprecated TLS versions weakens security; prefer upgrading the endpoint and scope any exception narrowly.
items.symptoms: Secure-channel/handshake_failure or protocol-version errors on TLS calls.
items.rootCause: No common TLS protocol version or cipher suite between client and server.
items.diagnosticCommand: openssl s_client -connect host.example.local:443 -tls1_2 ; repeat with -tls1_3 to see negotiation.
items.knownFix: Enable a mutually supported secure protocol/cipher or upgrade the endpoint; align TLS policy.
items.preventionRule: Standardize on TLS 1.2+; inventory endpoints that cannot meet it.
items.detectBy: SSL/TLS secure-channel or handshake errors.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: TLS protocol/cipher negotiation, not a certificate-trust bug.

items.title: HSTS and mixed-content blocking after enabling HTTPS
items.description: Symptoms: after enabling HTTPS, browsers refuse to load http resources (mixed content blocked), or users are stuck on https with no way back during testing because HSTS was cached. Root cause: pages reference http:// assets that the browser blocks on an https page, or Strict-Transport-Security with a long max-age (and includeSubDomains/preload) was sent prematurely and cached. Diagnostic: check the browser console for mixed-content warnings and inspect the Strict-Transport-Security response header. Known fix: serve all assets over https (or protocol-relative), and set HSTS max-age conservatively until HTTPS is proven, avoiding preload during rollout. Prevention rule: validate HTTPS fully before enabling long-lived HSTS; never preload until certain. LocalAIFactory detection: mixed-content blocks or unexpected forced-https from cached HSTS. Fix/warn: warn this is HSTS/mixed-content config, not a routing bug.
items.applicability: Apps migrating to HTTPS or enabling HSTS behind IIS/Kestrel.
items.example: An asset loaded over http is blocked on an https page; switching the asset URL to https clears the mixed-content error.
items.limitation: HSTS is cached by the browser and hard to undo; test on a throwaway host or short max-age first.
items.symptoms: Mixed-content blocked assets or forced-https stickiness from cached HSTS.
items.rootCause: http asset references on https pages, or premature long-max-age HSTS.
items.diagnosticCommand: Inspect the Strict-Transport-Security response header and browser console mixed-content warnings.
items.knownFix: Serve all assets over https, set a conservative HSTS max-age, avoid preload during rollout.
items.preventionRule: Validate HTTPS fully before long-lived HSTS; never preload until certain.
items.detectBy: Mixed-content blocks or unexpected forced-https from cached HSTS.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: HSTS/mixed-content config, not a routing bug.

items.title: Supply-chain / SBOM and reproducible-build gaps
items.description: Symptoms: a deployed artifact cannot be tied back to its source/inputs, a newly disclosed dependency vulnerability cannot be confirmed present, or two builds of the 'same' commit differ. Root cause: dependencies are floating (unpinned), no SBOM is produced, and no checksums tie the artifact to its inputs, so provenance is unverifiable. Diagnostic: compare the deployed artifact's checksum against the build record and generate/inspect an SBOM for the dependency in question. Known fix: pin dependency versions, generate an SBOM during build, publish artifact checksums, and build from a clean known source. Prevention rule: every release carries a pinned dependency set, an SBOM, and verifiable checksums. LocalAIFactory detection: missing checksum/SBOM for a deployed artifact when a CVE lands. Fix/warn: warn this is a provenance/supply-chain gap, not a runtime bug.
items.applicability: Regulated or security-sensitive releases requiring artifact provenance and dependency transparency.
items.example: When a dependency CVE is announced, the SBOM and pinned versions let the team confirm in minutes whether the release is affected.
items.limitation: An SBOM lists components but does not assess their risk; pair it with vulnerability monitoring.
items.symptoms: Unverifiable artifact provenance, unconfirmable dependency CVE, or non-reproducible builds.
items.rootCause: Unpinned dependencies, no SBOM, and no checksum linking artifact to inputs.
items.diagnosticCommand: sha256sum <artifact> and compare to the published build checksum; generate/inspect the SBOM for the affected package.
items.knownFix: Pin versions, generate an SBOM at build, publish artifact checksums, build from a clean source.
items.preventionRule: Every release carries pinned dependencies, an SBOM, and verifiable checksums.
items.detectBy: Missing checksum/SBOM for a deployed artifact when a CVE lands.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: provenance/supply-chain gap, not a runtime bug.

items.title: Backup RPO/RTO not met (recovery slower or staler than promised)
items.description: Symptoms: a restore drill loses more data than the stated recovery point objective (RPO) or takes longer than the recovery time objective (RTO); or restores fail outright. Root cause: backup frequency is too low for the RPO, log backups are missing so point-in-time recovery is impossible, backups are untested, or they are stored only on the same host that could be lost. Diagnostic: perform a timed test restore to a scratch instance and measure actual data loss and elapsed time against RPO/RTO. Known fix: align full/differential/log backup cadence to the RPO, store copies off-host, and rehearse restores to validate RTO. Prevention rule: test restores on a schedule; an untested backup is an assumption, not a recovery plan. LocalAIFactory detection: restore-drill metrics breaching RPO/RTO. Fix/warn: warn this is backup cadence/placement/testing, not a database engine bug.
items.applicability: Any deployment with data-loss tolerances, especially SQL Server primary stores in regulated estates.
items.example: A drill loses an hour of data because only nightly full backups exist; adding frequent log backups brings RPO within target.
items.limitation: RPO/RTO targets are business decisions; technical changes only meet targets that have actually been set.
items.symptoms: Restore exceeds RTO, loses more than RPO, or fails.
items.rootCause: Insufficient backup frequency, missing log backups, untested or on-host-only backups.
items.diagnosticCommand: Perform a timed test restore to a scratch instance; measure actual data loss and elapsed time vs RPO/RTO.
items.knownFix: Align full/differential/log cadence to RPO, store copies off-host, rehearse restores for RTO.
items.preventionRule: Test restores on a schedule; an untested backup is not a recovery plan.
items.detectBy: Restore-drill metrics breaching RPO/RTO.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: backup cadence/placement/testing, not a database engine bug.

items.title: OAuth2 token expiry and refresh handling for API clients
items.description: Symptoms: an integration works for a while then starts returning 401, or every Nth call fails as a token rotates. Root cause: the client caches an access token past its exp without refreshing, does not handle a 401 by refreshing once, or refreshes too aggressively and hits the IdP rate limit. Diagnostic: log token exp and acquisition time; correlate 401s with token age. Known fix: refresh proactively before exp with a safety margin, refresh-and-retry once on a 401, and reuse a single token across calls within its lifetime. Prevention rule: treat tokens as short-lived and centralize acquisition/refresh with a margin. LocalAIFactory detection: periodic 401s correlated with token age. Fix/warn: warn this is token-lifecycle handling, not a credentials bug.
items.applicability: Service-to-service integrations using OAuth2 client-credentials or refresh tokens.
items.example: Calls 401 every hour because the cached token expired; refreshing 60 seconds before exp removes the failures.
items.limitation: IdP token lifetimes and refresh policies vary; verify against the provider.
items.symptoms: Periodic 401s as a cached token expires or rotates.
items.rootCause: Token cached past exp, no refresh-on-401, or over-aggressive refresh.
items.diagnosticCommand: Log token exp and acquisition time; correlate 401 responses with token age.
items.knownFix: Refresh before exp with a margin, refresh-and-retry once on 401, reuse the token within its lifetime.
items.preventionRule: Treat tokens as short-lived; centralize acquisition/refresh with a safety margin.
items.detectBy: Periodic 401s correlated with token age.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: token-lifecycle handling, not a credentials bug.

items.title: API rate-limiting / 429 without backoff handling
items.description: Symptoms: a third-party integration intermittently fails with HTTP 429 Too Many Requests, especially during bulk syncs, and naive retries make it worse. Root cause: the client exceeds the provider's rate limit and either does not read the Retry-After header or retries immediately without backoff, amplifying the load. Diagnostic: inspect 429 responses for Retry-After and any X-RateLimit-* headers; measure request rate against the documented limit. Known fix: honor Retry-After, apply exponential backoff with jitter, and throttle the client to stay under the limit. Prevention rule: rate-limit the client to the provider's quota and always back off on 429. LocalAIFactory detection: 429 bursts during bulk operations. Fix/warn: warn this is client-side throttling/backoff, not a provider outage.
items.applicability: Integrations performing bulk reads/writes against rate-limited third-party APIs (commerce, ERP, identity).
items.example: A bulk product sync gets 429s; honoring Retry-After and adding jittered backoff completes the sync reliably.
items.limitation: Provider limits and headers differ; verify the specific API's documented quota and headers.
items.symptoms: Intermittent 429 during bulk operations.
items.rootCause: Exceeding the rate limit and retrying without honoring Retry-After/backoff.
items.diagnosticCommand: Inspect 429 responses for Retry-After and X-RateLimit-* headers; compare request rate to the documented limit.
items.knownFix: Honor Retry-After, apply exponential backoff with jitter, throttle to stay under the limit.
items.preventionRule: Rate-limit the client to the provider's quota; always back off on 429.
items.detectBy: 429 bursts during bulk operations.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: client-side throttling/backoff, not a provider outage.

items.title: Kestrel/IIS request timeout vs long-running request
items.description: Symptoms: long operations are cut off with 502/504 or a connection reset after a fixed interval, while the work itself would have completed. Root cause: a request that does heavy synchronous work exceeds the IIS/ARR request timeout or the proxy idle timeout, and the response is terminated mid-flight. Diagnostic: correlate the failure time with the configured timeout and the request-timing logs (a 'started' with no matching 'completed' near the cutoff). Known fix: move long work off the request path to a background job/queue and return quickly, or raise the relevant timeout only where genuinely needed. Prevention rule: keep request handlers short; never do long synchronous work on the request path. LocalAIFactory detection: 502/504 at a consistent elapsed time. Fix/warn: warn to offload the work, not to merely extend timeouts.
items.applicability: Apps with import/report/long-compute endpoints behind IIS/ARR or a reverse proxy.
items.example: A synchronous import 504s at the proxy timeout; moving it to the ingestion background queue returns immediately and completes asynchronously.
items.limitation: Some long operations legitimately need a longer timeout; offloading is preferred but not always possible.
items.symptoms: 502/504 or reset at a consistent elapsed time on long requests.
items.rootCause: Long synchronous work exceeding the IIS/ARR/proxy timeout.
items.diagnosticCommand: Correlate the failure elapsed time with the configured timeout and the request-timing 'started/completed' logs.
items.knownFix: Offload long work to a background job/queue and return quickly, or raise the timeout only where needed.
items.preventionRule: Keep request handlers short; no long synchronous work on the request path.
items.detectBy: 502/504 at a consistent elapsed time.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: offload the work rather than merely extend timeouts.

items.title: PrestaShop webservice 401/disabled or key permission failure
items.description: Symptoms: PrestaShop webservice calls return 401 Unauthorized, 'Webservice is disabled', or 403 on a resource. Root cause: the webservice is not enabled in Advanced Parameters, the API key lacks permission on the requested resource, or the key is sent incorrectly (HTTP Basic with the key as username and empty password) without HTTPS. Diagnostic: GET /api/ with the key over HTTPS to list permitted resources. Known fix: enable the webservice, grant the key resource permissions, and send it as Basic auth username over HTTPS. Prevention rule: provision least-privilege keys per resource and require HTTPS. LocalAIFactory detection: 401/403 or webservice-disabled responses. Fix/warn: warn this is webservice enablement/key permission, not a catalog bug.
items.applicability: Integrations reading or writing PrestaShop data via its webservice API.
items.example: A call 403s because the key lacks the products resource; granting it resolves the call.
items.limitation: PrestaShop versions differ in webservice behavior; verify on the store.
items.symptoms: 401 Unauthorized, webservice disabled, or 403 on a resource.
items.rootCause: Webservice disabled, missing key permission, or key sent without HTTPS.
items.diagnosticCommand: curl -s -u <key>: https://shop.example.local/api/ to list permitted resources over HTTPS.
items.knownFix: Enable the webservice, grant the key resource permissions, send as Basic auth over HTTPS.
items.preventionRule: Least-privilege per-resource keys; require HTTPS.
items.detectBy: 401/403 or webservice-disabled responses.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: webservice enablement/key permission, not a catalog bug.

items.title: Joomla web services / token and route 404 failures
items.description: Symptoms: Joomla web-service calls return 401, 403, or 404 on /api routes. Root cause: the API Authentication plugin (token) is not enabled, the user's API token is missing/invalid, or the /api application route is not reachable (rewrite not configured). Diagnostic: GET /api/index.php/v1/ with the X-Joomla-Token header to confirm auth and routing. Known fix: enable the token plugin, issue a token to a permitted user, send the X-Joomla-Token header, and ensure /api routing/rewrite works. Prevention rule: use least-privilege users with API tokens and verify /api routing. LocalAIFactory detection: 401/403/404 on Joomla /api. Fix/warn: warn this is token/plugin/routing, not a content bug.
items.applicability: Integrations using Joomla 4+ web services to read or write content.
items.example: Calls 404 because /api rewrite is missing on the server; configuring it makes the routes reachable.
items.limitation: Joomla web-service surface and plugins vary by version/extensions; verify on the site.
items.symptoms: 401/403/404 on Joomla /api routes.
items.rootCause: Token plugin disabled, invalid token, or /api routing not configured.
items.diagnosticCommand: curl -s -H 'X-Joomla-Token: <token>' https://joomla.example.local/api/index.php/v1/
items.knownFix: Enable the token plugin, issue a token to a permitted user, send X-Joomla-Token, fix /api routing.
items.preventionRule: Least-privilege users with API tokens; verify /api routing.
items.detectBy: 401/403/404 on Joomla /api.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: token/plugin/routing, not a content bug.

items.title: Webhook delivery failure (signature/timeout/retry storms)
items.description: Symptoms: inbound webhooks from a provider are rejected (signature invalid), time out, or arrive duplicated as the provider retries. Root cause: the signature is verified against the wrong secret or a transformed body, the handler does slow work before acknowledging so the provider times out and retries, or the endpoint is not idempotent so retries double-process. Diagnostic: log the raw body and computed vs received signature; measure handler ack latency. Known fix: verify the signature over the exact raw payload, acknowledge fast and process asynchronously, and dedupe by event id for idempotency. Prevention rule: validate signatures on the raw body, ack within the provider's window, and make handlers idempotent. LocalAIFactory detection: signature rejects, webhook timeouts, or duplicate processing. Fix/warn: warn this is signature/ack-latency/idempotency, not a provider bug.
items.applicability: Endpoints receiving webhooks from commerce, payment, or identity providers.
items.example: A provider retries because the handler took too long to ack; acking immediately and processing on a queue stops the duplicates.
items.limitation: Each provider's signing scheme and retry policy differs; verify the provider's documentation.
items.symptoms: Signature rejects, webhook timeouts, or duplicate processing.
items.rootCause: Wrong-secret/body signature, slow ack causing retries, or non-idempotent handler.
items.diagnosticCommand: Log the raw request body and compare the computed HMAC to the received signature; measure ack latency.
items.knownFix: Verify signature over the raw body, ack fast and process async, dedupe by event id.
items.preventionRule: Validate on raw body, ack within the window, make handlers idempotent.
items.detectBy: Signature rejects, timeouts, or duplicate processing.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: signature/ack-latency/idempotency, not a provider bug.

items.title: IIS application pool recycling drops in-flight work
items.description: Symptoms: periodic brief errors, lost in-memory state, or interrupted long requests at regular intervals or under memory pressure. Root cause: the IIS app pool recycles on its default schedule (e.g. periodic restart, idle timeout, or memory limit), terminating in-flight requests and clearing in-process state. Diagnostic: check the app pool recycling settings and correlate error timestamps with recycle events in the event log. Known fix: tune the recycle schedule, disable idle timeout for always-on workloads, and never rely on in-process memory for durable state; use the database or a queue. Prevention rule: design for recycle-at-any-time; persist state and offload long work. LocalAIFactory detection: errors clustering at recycle intervals. Fix/warn: warn this is app-pool recycle behavior, not an app crash.
items.applicability: ASP.NET Core apps hosted in-process under IIS that keep state in memory or run long requests.
items.example: In-memory cache evaporates every 29 hours at the default periodic recycle; moving durable state to the database removes the impact.
items.limitation: Disabling recycling entirely can mask leaks; tune rather than disable, and fix the leak if one exists.
items.symptoms: Periodic errors, lost in-memory state, or interrupted requests at intervals.
items.rootCause: App pool recycling on schedule/idle/memory, terminating in-flight work and in-process state.
items.diagnosticCommand: Review app pool recycling/idle-timeout settings; correlate error times with recycle events in the Windows event log.
items.knownFix: Tune the recycle schedule, disable idle timeout for always-on apps, persist state outside the process.
items.preventionRule: Design for recycle-at-any-time; persist state and offload long work.
items.detectBy: Errors clustering at recycle intervals.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: app-pool recycle behavior, not an app crash.

items.title: Data Protection keys not persisted (antiforgery/cookie breaks on restart)
items.description: Symptoms: after an app restart or across instances, users are logged out, antiforgery tokens fail with 'unable to unprotect', or encrypted values cannot be decrypted. Root cause: ASP.NET Core Data Protection keys are stored only in memory or in a per-instance location, so a restart or a second instance cannot decrypt what a previous key protected. Diagnostic: check whether the key ring is persisted to a shared, durable location and whether instances share it. Known fix: persist keys to a shared directory (or other shared store) and protect them at rest, so all instances and restarts use the same key ring. Prevention rule: configure a persistent, shared Data Protection key store before scaling out or relying on encrypted cookies. LocalAIFactory detection: 'unable to unprotect' or mass logout after restart. Fix/warn: warn this is Data Protection key persistence, not an auth-logic bug.
items.applicability: ASP.NET Core apps using antiforgery, auth cookies, or Data Protection-encrypted values, especially multi-instance or frequently restarted.
items.example: Users are logged out on every deploy because keys lived in memory; persisting the key ring to a shared path stops it. This mirrors LocalAIFactory storing keys under a dedicated keys/ directory.
items.limitation: The shared key store must itself be secured and backed up; losing it loses the ability to decrypt protected data.
items.symptoms: Logout on restart, antiforgery 'unable to unprotect', or undecryptable values across instances.
items.rootCause: Data Protection keys not persisted to a shared durable store.
items.diagnosticCommand: Confirm the Data Protection key ring path is persistent and shared; check logs for 'unable to unprotect the message'.
items.knownFix: Persist keys to a shared directory/store protected at rest so all instances/restarts share the key ring.
items.preventionRule: Configure a persistent, shared key store before scaling out or relying on encrypted cookies.
items.detectBy: 'Unable to unprotect' errors or mass logout after restart.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: Data Protection key persistence, not an auth-logic bug.

items.title: Salesforce REST API session/limit and field-level errors
items.description: Symptoms: Salesforce REST calls return INVALID_SESSION_ID, REQUEST_LIMIT_EXCEEDED, or INVALID_FIELD/insufficient access. Root cause: the OAuth session/access token expired without refresh, the org's daily API request limit was hit, or the integration user lacks field-level security/object permission. Diagnostic: GET /services/data/ to confirm the token and API version, then query a permitted object. Known fix: refresh the token, throttle to stay under the org API limit, and grant the integration user the needed object/field permissions. Prevention rule: refresh proactively, monitor API usage against the org limit, and use a least-privilege integration user. LocalAIFactory detection: INVALID_SESSION_ID or REQUEST_LIMIT_EXCEEDED. Fix/warn: warn this is session/limit/permission, not a query bug.
items.applicability: Integrations reading or writing Salesforce data via the REST API.
items.example: Calls fail INVALID_FIELD because field-level security hides a field from the integration user; granting access resolves it.
items.limitation: Salesforce API limits and permission models are org-specific; verify on the org.
items.symptoms: INVALID_SESSION_ID, REQUEST_LIMIT_EXCEEDED, or INVALID_FIELD/access errors.
items.rootCause: Expired token, org API limit reached, or missing object/field permission.
items.diagnosticCommand: curl -s -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' https://<instance>.salesforce.com/services/data/
items.knownFix: Refresh the token, throttle under the org API limit, grant object/field permissions.
items.preventionRule: Refresh proactively, monitor API usage, use a least-privilege integration user.
items.detectBy: INVALID_SESSION_ID or REQUEST_LIMIT_EXCEEDED responses.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: session/limit/permission, not a query bug.

items.title: ServiceNow Table API auth and query/pagination failures
items.description: Symptoms: ServiceNow Table API calls return 401, 403 (ACL/role), or only the first batch of records. Root cause: Basic/OAuth credentials are wrong or the user lacks the table ACL/role, or the client ignores sysparm_limit/sysparm_offset and the Link header so it stops after the first page. Diagnostic: GET /api/now/table/<table>?sysparm_limit=1 with credentials to confirm access, then page using sysparm_offset and the Link header. Known fix: use a least-privilege integration user with the needed role, and paginate with sysparm_limit/sysparm_offset following the Link header. Prevention rule: provision a dedicated integration role and always paginate. LocalAIFactory detection: 401/403 or single-page ServiceNow result sets. Fix/warn: warn this is credentials/role or pagination, not a data bug.
items.applicability: Integrations reading or writing ServiceNow records via the Table API.
items.example: A pull returns 10000 of 50000 records because pagination headers were ignored; following the Link header retrieves all.
items.limitation: ServiceNow roles/ACLs and instance config vary; verify on the instance.
items.symptoms: 401, 403 ACL/role, or single-page ServiceNow result sets.
items.rootCause: Wrong credentials/missing role, or pagination (sysparm_limit/offset, Link header) ignored.
items.diagnosticCommand: curl -s -u user:pass 'https://<instance>.service-now.com/api/now/table/incident?sysparm_limit=1'
items.knownFix: Use a least-privilege role and paginate with sysparm_limit/sysparm_offset following the Link header.
items.preventionRule: Dedicated integration role; always paginate.
items.detectBy: 401/403 or single-page ServiceNow result sets.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: credentials/role or pagination, not a data bug.

items.title: OpenCart API session token and route failures
items.description: Symptoms: OpenCart API calls fail to obtain a session, return a login error, or 404 on a route. Root cause: the API user/key is wrong or disabled, the api_token/session is not passed on subsequent calls, or the storefront/admin API route is not enabled. Diagnostic: POST to the login route to obtain a token, then pass it on follow-up calls. Known fix: enable the API user, log in to get the token, and include it on every subsequent request. Prevention rule: provision a dedicated API user and carry the session token through the flow. LocalAIFactory detection: login errors or 404 on API routes. Fix/warn: warn this is API user/session/route, not a catalog bug.
items.applicability: Integrations using the OpenCart API for cart/order operations.
items.example: Calls fail because the api_token from login was not forwarded; including it on each request fixes the flow.
items.limitation: OpenCart versions and custom API extensions vary widely; verify on the store.
items.symptoms: No session, login error, or 404 on API routes.
items.rootCause: Wrong/disabled API user, token not forwarded, or route not enabled.
items.diagnosticCommand: POST the OpenCart API login route to obtain api_token; then include it on subsequent calls.
items.knownFix: Enable the API user, obtain the token via login, forward it on every request.
items.preventionRule: Dedicated API user; carry the session token through the flow.
items.detectBy: Login errors or 404 on API routes.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: API user/session/route, not a catalog bug.

items.title: Database deadlock under concurrent writes
items.description: Symptoms: intermittent 'Transaction (Process ID) was deadlocked on lock resources and has been chosen as the deadlock victim' (SQL error 1205), failing writes under concurrency. Root cause: two transactions acquire locks on the same resources in opposite order, or long transactions hold locks while contending. Diagnostic: capture the deadlock graph (Extended Events / trace flag) to see the resources and statement order. Known fix: order writes consistently across code paths, keep transactions short, add covering indexes to reduce lock footprint, and retry the victim with backoff. Prevention rule: consistent lock ordering, short transactions, and idempotent retry on 1205. LocalAIFactory detection: error 1205 spikes under concurrent writes. Fix/warn: warn this is lock-ordering/transaction-design, not a data-corruption bug.
items.applicability: Apps with concurrent write paths against SQL Server.
items.example: Two import workers update the same rows in opposite order and deadlock; normalizing the update order eliminates it.
items.limitation: Some deadlocks need schema/index changes; retry alone is a stopgap, not a fix.
items.symptoms: SQL error 1205 deadlock-victim failures under concurrency.
items.rootCause: Opposite-order lock acquisition or long transactions contending on the same resources.
items.diagnosticCommand: Capture the deadlock graph via Extended Events (system_health) to see resources and statement order.
items.knownFix: Consistent write ordering, short transactions, covering indexes, retry the victim with backoff.
items.preventionRule: Consistent lock ordering, short transactions, idempotent retry on 1205.
items.detectBy: Error 1205 spikes under concurrent writes.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: lock-ordering/transaction-design, not data corruption.

items.title: Certificate expiry / chain validation failure
items.description: Symptoms: HTTPS or service calls fail with 'certificate has expired', 'untrusted root', or 'name mismatch', often appearing suddenly. Root cause: the server certificate expired, an intermediate certificate is missing from the chain, the trust anchor is not installed on the client, or the hostname does not match the certificate SAN. Diagnostic: openssl s_client -connect host:443 -showcerts to inspect the chain, expiry, and SANs. Known fix: renew the certificate, serve the full intermediate chain, install the trust anchor on clients, and ensure the SAN matches the hostname. Prevention rule: monitor certificate expiry with alerting and serve the complete chain. LocalAIFactory detection: certificate-expired/untrusted/name-mismatch errors. Fix/warn: warn this is certificate lifecycle/chain/SAN, not a TLS protocol bug.
items.applicability: Any HTTPS endpoint or mutually-authenticated service call relying on certificates.
items.example: Calls suddenly fail because an intermediate certificate was dropped from the chain; serving the full chain restores trust.
items.limitation: Client trust-store specifics differ across OS/runtime; verify on the client platform.
items.symptoms: Certificate expired, untrusted root, or name-mismatch errors.
items.rootCause: Expired cert, missing intermediate, untrusted anchor, or SAN/hostname mismatch.
items.diagnosticCommand: openssl s_client -connect host.example.local:443 -showcerts ; inspect expiry, chain, and SANs.
items.knownFix: Renew the certificate, serve the full chain, install the trust anchor, match SAN to hostname.
items.preventionRule: Monitor certificate expiry with alerting; serve the complete chain.
items.detectBy: Certificate-expired/untrusted/name-mismatch errors.
items.fixOrWarn: Warn: certificate lifecycle/chain/SAN, not a TLS protocol bug.
